```yaml
  shared_store: filesystem
  retention_enabled: true
```

**`promtail-config.yml`** — Docker socket discovery:
```yaml
server:
  http_listen_port: 9080
  grpc_listen_port: 0
positions:
  filename: /tmp/positions.yaml
clients:
  - url: http://loki:3100/loki/api/v1/push
scrape_configs:
  - job_name: containers
    docker_sd_configs:
      - host: unix:///var/run/docker.sock
        refresh_interval: 5s
    relabel_configs:
      - source_labels: [__meta_docker_container_name]
        regex: /(.*)
        target_label: container
      - source_labels: [__meta_docker_container_log_stream]
        target_label: logstream
      - source_labels: [__meta_docker_container_label_com_docker_compose_service]
        target_label: service
```

### 3. Add services to gordon-stack compose (`docker-compose.yml`)

Add loki, promtail, and grafana services per the reference snippets in the decision doc (Section 5):
- **loki**: `grafana/loki:latest`, port `127.0.0.1:3100:3100`, volume `loki-data:/loki`, config mount, named volume in volumes section
- **promtail**: `grafana/promtail:latest`, mounts Docker socket + `/var/lib/docker/containers`, config mount
  - **T418 Windows/Docker Desktop note**: `/var/lib/docker/containers` is inside the Docker Desktop Linux VM — validate this mount works. Docker socket at `/var/run/docker.sock` should work.
- **grafana**: `grafana/grafana:latest`, port `127.0.0.1:3000:3000`, volume `grafana-data:/var/lib/grafana`, env var `GF_SECURITY_ADMIN_PASSWORD=${GRAFANA_ADMIN_PASSWORD}`, bind to `127.0.0.1` only (not internet-exposed until SSO)
- Add named volumes: `loki-data`, `grafana-data`
- All three services on the same internal docker network as other gordon-stack services

### 4. Add env vars to note (DO NOT write to .env — Enzo adds these)

Note in Handoff Log that Enzo must add to `02_Runtime/gordon-prod/.env`:
- `GRAFANA_ADMIN_PASSWORD` — admin password for Grafana UI
- `TELEGRAM_BOT_TOKEN` — for Grafana alert contact (add later in Grafana UI)
- `TELEGRAM_CHAT_ID` — for Grafana alert contact

### 5. Configure Grafana post-launch (manual steps for Enzo)

After `docker compose up -d loki promtail grafana`:
1. Open Grafana at `http://localhost:3000`
2. Add Loki datasource: URL = `http://loki:3100`
3. Add a Telegram Contact Point in Grafana Alerting → Contact Points
4. Import or create a basic log dashboard (LogQL query: `{container=~".+"}`)
5. Create an alert rule: any `level=error` log line → Telegram

### 6. Commit and document

- Create `01_Repos/gordon-stack/docs/observability-logs.md` documenting: services, ports, retention config, alert wiring instructions
- Commit to gordon-stack (local only — no remote): `feat: add Loki+Promtail+Grafana log stack to gordon-stack — TKT-0064`
- Identity: Nassau-1 / enzoterrier@gmail.com, no Claude co-author

## Execution Notes

- TKT-0062 decision doc is at `01_Repos/gordon-stack/docs/observability-stack-decision.md`.
- Grafana is shared with TKT-0065 (metrics stack) — do NOT deploy two Grafana containers. TKT-0065 will add Prometheus as a second datasource to the same Grafana.
- Bind Grafana to `127.0.0.1:3000` — do not expose via nginx until Keycloak SSO is ready.
- On T418 (Docker Desktop for Windows): validate that `/var/lib/docker/containers` Promtail mount actually provides log access. If not, use the Docker socket-based discovery in promtail-config.yml which is more reliable.
- This is a live service addition. Follow `00_Standards/runtime-change-control-checklist.md`.
- gordon-stack is local-only — commit but do not push.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] TKT-0062 decision doc read and task section of this ticket populated
- [ ] Chosen log management stack deployed in gordon-stack compose
- [ ] Container log ingestion working for all live services
- [ ] Error alert rule configured (Telegram notification on ERROR-level log pattern)
- [ ] Audit trail retention confirmed (90+ days)
- [ ] Deployment documented in gordon-stack docs

## Handoff Log
- 2026-04-08 | COO | Placeholder ticket created. Blocked on TKT-0062 evaluation. Task section intentionally empty — will be populated from TKT-0062 output. | Status: BLOCKED | Next: activate when TKT-0062 is DONE.

active.title: Deploy infra/fleet monitoring solution to T418 gordon-stack (per TKT-0062 decision)
active.created: Wed Apr 08 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Wed Apr 08 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context

T418 has no infrastructure or fleet-level monitoring (CPU, RAM, disk, container health metrics). TKT-0062 will evaluate and decide the tooling (Prometheus+Grafana vs Netdata vs alternatives) with the primary use case being fleet management — monitoring container and system-level resource usage across the gordon-stack. This ticket is a placeholder pending the TKT-0062 decision. Enzo noted this can be re-evaluated in future as needs evolve.

## Current State

- No infra/fleet monitoring deployed on T418
- TKT-0062 evaluation not yet complete
- This ticket has no task content yet — will be written once the tool is decided
- If Prometheus+Grafana is chosen for metrics AND Loki+Grafana for logs (TKT-0064), the two deployments should share a single Grafana instance — coordinate with TKT-0064 agent

## Task

**TKT-0062 DONE. Decision: Prometheus + cAdvisor + node_exporter (shared Grafana from TKT-0064).**
**Blocked on TKT-0064 — Grafana must be deployed first. Do not start until TKT-0064 is DONE.**

Read `01_Repos/gordon-stack/docs/observability-stack-decision.md` Section 2 and Section 3 before starting.

### 1. Prerequisites

- TKT-0064 must be DONE (Grafana is running at `http://localhost:3000`)
- Note the Grafana container name from TKT-0064 — reuse it; do NOT deploy a second Grafana

### 2. Create config file in gordon-stack repo

Create `01_Repos/gordon-stack/prometheus.yml`:
```yaml
global:
  scrape_interval: 15s
  evaluation_interval: 15s
scrape_configs:
  - job_name: 'cadvisor'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['cadvisor:8080']
  - job_name: 'node-exporter'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['node-exporter:9100']
  - job_name: 'prometheus'
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['localhost:9090']
```

### 3. Add services to gordon-stack compose

Add to `docker-compose.yml` per reference snippets in decision doc Section 5:

- **prometheus**: `prom/prometheus:latest`, port `127.0.0.1:9090:9090`, volume `prometheus-data:/prometheus`, config mount `./prometheus.yml`, flag `--storage.tsdb.retention.time=90d`
- **cadvisor**: `gcr.io/cadvisor/cadvisor:latest`, port `127.0.0.1:8080:8080`, mounts: Docker socket + `/sys:ro` + `/var/lib/docker/:ro`
  - **T418 Windows/Docker Desktop note (CRITICAL)**: `/sys` and `/var/lib/docker` mounts may be partial under Docker Desktop Linux VM. The Docker socket mount is the reliable path. Validate cAdvisor coverage after deployment and document actual vs expected metrics in this ticket's Handoff Log.
  - cAdvisor port 8080 conflicts with Keycloak if Keycloak is on 8080 — check service port registry before deploying
- **node-exporter**: `prom/node-exporter:latest`, port `127.0.0.1:9100:9100`, mounts: `/proc`, `/sys`, `/` with host path flags
  - **T418 Windows note**: `/proc` and `/sys` inside Docker Desktop VM = Linux VM metrics, not Windows host. Document actual coverage.
- Add named volume: `prometheus-data`
- All services on same internal network

### 4. Add Prometheus datasource to Grafana (manual step for Enzo)

After `docker compose up -d prometheus cadvisor node-exporter`:
1. Open Grafana at `http://localhost:3000`
2. Add Prometheus datasource: URL = `http://prometheus:9090`
3. Import dashboard: cAdvisor containers dashboard (Grafana ID: 14282) and Node Exporter Full (ID: 1860)
4. Create metric alert rules: disk usage `(1 - node_filesystem_avail_bytes/node_filesystem_size_bytes) > 0.8` and container restarts

### 5. Commit and document

- Create `01_Repos/gordon-stack/docs/observability-metrics.md` with: services, ports, known T418/Docker Desktop metric gaps, alert setup
- Commit to gordon-stack (local only): `feat: add Prometheus+cAdvisor+node_exporter metrics to gordon-stack — TKT-0065`
- Identity: Nassau-1 / enzoterrier@gmail.com, no Claude co-author

## Execution Notes

- Do NOT deploy until TKT-0064 is DONE and Grafana is running.
- Do NOT add a second Grafana container. Reuse the one from TKT-0064.
- cAdvisor default port is 8080 — check against Keycloak's port in service registry. If conflict, remap cAdvisor to a different host port (e.g., 8083) and update prometheus.yml accordingly.
- On T418 (Docker Desktop for Windows): /sys and /proc mounts reflect the Linux VM, not the Windows host. Host-level disk metrics will show VM disk, not full Windows disk layout. Accept and document this limitation — do not block the ticket on it.
- This is a live service addition. Follow `00_Standards/runtime-change-control-checklist.md`.
- gordon-stack is local-only — commit but do not push.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] TKT-0062 decision doc read and task section of this ticket populated
- [ ] Chosen fleet monitoring stack deployed in gordon-stack compose
- [ ] Container-level metrics visible (CPU, RAM, restart count per container)
- [ ] Host-level metrics visible (disk, load, memory)
- [ ] Alert configured for disk >80% and container crash (Telegram)
- [ ] Deployment documented in gordon-stack docs
- [ ] No duplicate Grafana containers (coordinate with TKT-0064 if Grafana shared)

## Handoff Log
- 2026-04-08 | COO | Placeholder ticket created. Blocked on TKT-0062 evaluation. Task section intentionally empty. Note: if Prometheus+Grafana chosen, share Grafana instance with TKT-0064 (log management). | Status: BLOCKED | Next: activate when TKT-0062 is DONE.
- 2026-04-09 | COO | TKT-0062 DONE. Decision: Prometheus + cAdvisor + node_exporter; shared Grafana from TKT-0064. Task section populated. blocked_by updated: TKT-0062 → TKT-0064 (Grafana must exist first). Status remains BLOCKED on TKT-0064. | Status: BLOCKED on TKT-0064 | Next: start after TKT-0064 completes and Grafana is confirmed running. | cAdvisor port 8080 may conflict with Keycloak — check registry before deploying.
- 2026-04-09 | Engineering Core (TKT-0065 session) | Compose and config implemented. prometheus.yml created (scrape_interval 15s; targets: cadvisor:8080, node-exporter:9100, prometheus self). docker-compose.yml updated: prometheus (v2.49.1, host 9090), cadvisor (v0.47.2, host 8083 — 8080 is owned by Keycloak, confirmed in service-port-registry.md Port Conflict Analysis), node-exporter (v1.7.0, host 9100). prometheus-data named volume added. service-port-registry.md updated with all three services and changelog entry. docs/observability-metrics.md created with stack overview, port assignments, T418/Docker Desktop limitation documentation, and post-launch steps for Enzo. Committed locally to gordon-stack (commit aa180c4) — not pushed (local-only repo). No Grafana container added (shared instance from TKT-0064). | Status: IN_REVIEW | Current state: Compose READY. Services NOT yet running. TKT-0064 (Grafana) must be running before adding Prometheus datasource. | What comes next: (1) Ensure TKT-0064 is running (loki, promtail, grafana), (2) `docker compose up -d prometheus cadvisor node-exporter`, (3) Add Prometheus datasource in Grafana UI: URL = http://prometheus:9090, (4) Import Grafana dashboards ID 14282 (cAdvisor) and ID 1860 (Node Exporter Full), (5) Wire PromQL alert rules (disk >80%, container restarts) to Telegram contact point. | Pitfalls: /sys and /proc metrics = Docker Desktop Linux VM only, not full Windows host. cAdvisor filesystem coverage may be partial — validate after launch via Prometheus Targets page. Do NOT add a second Grafana container — it is from TKT-0064.

active.title: Deploy Open WebUI as T418 gordon-stack sidecar (infra prerequisite for ZO integration)
active.created: Wed Apr 08 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Thu Apr 09 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context

TKT-0059 evaluates how Open WebUI (https://github.com/open-webui/open-webui) will be embedded in the movecapital-platform as the ZO assistant main page. TKT-0060 amends the ZO architecture and re-scopes TKT-0045. But neither ticket includes the raw infrastructure step of running Open WebUI as a container on T418 within gordon-stack. This is a prerequisite: TKT-0045 (re-scoped ZO service) and the platform integration cannot be built or tested until Open WebUI is actually running on T418. This ticket is blocked on TKT-0059 completing its adoption recommendation — if Open WebUI is NOT adopted, this ticket is closed with no action.

## Current State

- Open WebUI is not deployed on T418
- gordon-stack compose is at `01_Repos/gordon-stack/` (local-only, not GitHub-backed)
- Live runtime root: `02_Runtime/gordon-prod/`
- Port **8085** is assigned to Open WebUI (resolved 2026-04-09 by TKT-0069 service registry). Port 8080 = Keycloak, 8084 = comps-pipeline-worker, 8085 is clean. Port 3001 = Uptime Kuma (already live).
- TKT-0059 evaluation not yet complete — this ticket activates only if TKT-0059 recommends adoption

## Task

**Activate only after TKT-0059 recommendation is "Adopt" or "Adopt partially".**

Read TKT-0059's output doc (`01_Repos/zo/app/docs/design/open-webui-zo-evaluation.md`) before starting. The embedding approach (Option A/B/C) chosen in TKT-0059 affects whether Open WebUI needs to be reverse-proxied or accessed directly.

1. **Scope the change** using `00_Standards/runtime-change-control-checklist.md`.

2. **Add Open WebUI service to gordon-stack compose**:
   - Image: `ghcr.io/open-webui/open-webui:main` (or the version pinned in TKT-0059's recommendation)
   - Persistent volume for Open WebUI data (`/app/backend/data`) — place in `02_Runtime/gordon-prod/open-webui-data/`
   - Port: **8085** (assigned by TKT-0069 service registry — do not change)
   - Restart policy: `unless-stopped`
   - Environment variables: API keys (Anthropic, OpenRouter, Ollama endpoint) — reference from `02_Runtime/gordon-prod/.env`; do not hard-code

3. **Configure model backends** (in Open WebUI admin on first launch):
   - Anthropic Claude API — claude-sonnet-4-6 as default (confirm model from ZO architecture doc)
   - Ollama endpoint — if a local Ollama service is running on T418; skip if not
   - OpenRouter — if required per TKT-0059 recommendation
   - Do not configure models that require API keys not already in the stack

4. **Configure authentication**:
   - If TKT-0042 (Keycloak realm hardening) is DONE: wire Open WebUI to Keycloak OIDC
   - If TKT-0042 is not yet done: configure Open WebUI's built-in user management as a temporary measure; note this as `TEMP_AUTH` in the Handoff Log; a follow-up wiring task must be created

5. **Verify deployment**:
   - `docker ps` confirms `open-webui` container running
   - UI accessible at assigned port
   - At least one model (Claude via API) responds to a test message
   - Data volume persists across `docker compose restart open-webui`

6. **Document** in `01_Repos/gordon-stack/docs/open-webui.md`:
   - Container name, assigned port
   - Volume path in `02_Runtime/gordon-prod/`
   - Model backends configured
   - Auth status (Keycloak wired or temp auth)
   - API endpoint used by TKT-0045 (ZO service integration)

## Execution Notes

- This is a **live service addition**. Follow `00_Standards/runtime-change-control-checklist.md`.
- gordon-stack is local-only on T418 — no push to GitHub.
- Do not print or echo `.env` contents. Only confirm API key entries exist.
- Open WebUI's data volume must be in `02_Runtime/gordon-prod/` — not in the repo.
- If Ollama is not running on T418, skip Ollama config entirely — do not install Ollama as part of this ticket.
- If TKT-0059 recommended Option A (iframe) or Option B (nginx subpath proxy): note the required nginx config changes in the Handoff Log but do not implement them here — create a follow-on ticket for nginx changes.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Open WebUI container running in gordon-stack compose
- [ ] At least one model backend (Claude API) responding
- [ ] Data volume persistent across restarts
- [ ] Auth configured (Keycloak or temp — documented in Handoff Log)
- [ ] Deployment documented in `01_Repos/gordon-stack/docs/open-webui.md`
- [ ] Port assignment noted and free from conflicts with existing services
- [ ] TKT-0045 can proceed (ZO service has a running Open WebUI endpoint to integrate with)

## Handoff Log
- 2026-04-08 | COO | Ticket created from session gap analysis. Infra prerequisite for TKT-0045 (ZO service integration). Blocked on TKT-0059 adoption decision — if not adopted, close this ticket with no action. | Status: BLOCKED | Next: activate when TKT-0059 is DONE and adoption is confirmed.
- 2026-04-08 | COO (TKT-0060) | Unblocked. TKT-0059 DONE — decision: Adopt partially, Option C (headless Open WebUI + ZO Middleware). Open WebUI UI is NOT exposed — backend only. Port: avoid 3000, 3001 (Uptime Kuma), check compose for free port. Auth: wire to Keycloak if TKT-0042 is DONE; otherwise use temp auth and create follow-up wiring ticket. | Status: OPEN, no blockers. | Assign to Backend Architect. This is the next infrastructure ticket in WS-ASSISTANT. | Reminder: TKT-0045 (ZO Middleware) is still blocked on Phase 5 gate — TKT-0066 can be deployed to gordon-stack now but ZO Middleware won't be wired to it until Phase 6.
- 2026-04-09 | Engineering Core (TKT-0066 session) | Open WebUI service added to gordon-stack compose (`01_Repos/gordon-stack/docker-compose.yml`). Service: `open-webui`, image `ghcr.io/open-webui/open-webui:main`, port `127.0.0.1:8085:8080`, named volume `open-webui-data` at `/app/backend/data`, restart `unless-stopped`, network `n8n_network`. TEMP_AUTH configured (built-in Open WebUI auth, `ENABLE_SIGNUP=false`, `WEBUI_AUTH=true`) — Keycloak OIDC wiring deferred to TKT-0042 completion. `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY` and `WEBUI_SECRET_KEY` referenced from `.env`. ZO Middleware (TKT-0045) internal endpoint: `http://open-webui:8080`. Documentation created at `01_Repos/gordon-stack/docs/open-webui.md`. Service port registry updated — status PLANNED → DEPLOYED (not yet started). Committed locally (commit 3819f90) — no push (gordon-stack is local-only). | Current state: compose file updated and committed locally. Open WebUI is NOT yet running — Enzo must run `docker compose up -d open-webui` to activate. WEBUI_SECRET_KEY must be added to `02_Runtime/gordon-prod/.env` before starting (`openssl rand -hex 32`). After first launch, admin setup required in Open WebUI UI at http://localhost:8085: create admin account, configure Anthropic Claude API and Ollama connections. | What comes next: (1) Enzo adds WEBUI_SECRET_KEY to .env, (2) Enzo runs docker compose up -d open-webui, (3) admin setup in Open WebUI UI at http://localhost:8085, (4) verify claude-sonnet-4-6 responds to test message, (5) verify data persists across restart, (6) after TKT-0042 DONE: wire Keycloak OIDC to Open WebUI (env vars in open-webui-zo-evaluation.md §1.5), (7) TKT-0045 (ZO Middleware) can proceed once Open WebUI confirmed running. | Pitfalls: WEBUI_SECRET_KEY must be in .env before container starts or it will use an insecure default. Do NOT add nginx proxy_pass for port 8085 — Open WebUI UI is not the end-user surface. Do not expose port 8085 publicly until ZO Middleware (TKT-0045) is built and the exposure model is settled. Port 8085 is bound to 127.0.0.1 only (localhost-only binding in compose). | Status: IN_REVIEW

active.title: Implement ZO drawer UI — context-aware right-panel assistant component in movecapital-platform shell
active.created: Wed Apr 08 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Wed Apr 08 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context

ZO has two UI surfaces in movecapital-platform: (1) the main `/app/assistant` page (handled by TKT-0045, embedding Open WebUI per TKT-0059/TKT-0060 decision), and (2) the **ZO drawer** — a collapsible right-panel overlay accessible from any page in the platform shell. The drawer is context-aware: when opened on `/companies/[slug]`, it pre-populates the company context; on `/analysis/[run_id]`, it pre-populates the run context. The drawer connects to the same ZO backend (Open WebUI API or custom ZO service, per TKT-0060 decision) and shares conversation history with the main assistant page.

This ticket implements the drawer as a React component in the movecapital-platform Next.js shell. It is blocked until TKT-0060 settles the architecture (so the drawer calls the correct API) and TKT-0066 has Open WebUI running (so the backend exists to test against).

The drawer concept and UX spec are defined in `01_Repos/zo/app/docs/design/zo-assistant-architecture.md` Section 2.1. Read it in full before starting.

## Current State

- Platform shell at `01_Repos/zo/app/` — Next.js app, port 3100
- No ZO drawer component exists yet
- ZO architecture doc specifies the drawer as a collapsible right panel with a `[ZO]` button in the top-right of the shell
- Context injection spec: drawer must receive current page's entity context (company_id, deal_id, run_id depending on route)
- History sharing spec: drawer conversation history must be retrievable on the main `/app/assistant` page
- TKT-0060 will define the exact API the drawer calls — do not build against an assumed API until TKT-0060 is DONE

## Task

**Read TKT-0060's amended architecture before writing any code.** The API endpoints and auth model for the drawer are defined there.

### 1. Drawer shell component

Build `components/zo/ZODrawer.tsx` (or equivalent path per the platform's component structure):

- Collapsible right panel, triggered by a `[ZO]` button in the platform shell top-right
- Drawer width: ~380px (adjustable via design review)
- Overlay mode: does not shift main content; floats over it with a semi-transparent backdrop
- Persistent open/close state in React context (survives route navigation within the app)
- Keyboard shortcut: `Cmd/Ctrl + Shift + Z` to toggle

### 2. Context injection

The drawer must detect the current route and inject entity context into each message payload sent to the ZO backend:

| Route pattern | Context injected |
|---|---|
| `/companies/[slug]` | `{ entity_type: 'company', company_id, company_name }` |
| `/deals/[id]` | `{ entity_type: 'deal', deal_id, deal_name }` |
| `/analysis/[run_id]` | `{ entity_type: 'analysis_run', run_id }` |
| `/transcripts/[id]` | `{ entity_type: 'transcript', transcript_id }` |
| All other routes | `{ entity_type: null }` — no entity context |

The context object is appended to the ZO API message payload as defined in TKT-0060's amended architecture spec.

### 3. Suggested actions (quick chips)

When entity context is present, the drawer surface shows suggested action chips above the input:

| Context | Suggested chips |
|---|---|
| Company page | `Run comps`, `Show recent transcripts`, `Run seller docs factpack` |
| Analysis run | `Summarise run`, `Show sources`, `Compare to similar deals` |
| No context | `Open deals`, `Latest transcripts`, `Search companies` |

Chips fire pre-formed payloads to the ZO backend (same format as manual chat messages, but structured).

### 4. Conversation history linking

**Confirmed behaviour (Enzo, 2026-04-08)**: Drawer starts a fresh conversation per page visit (new chat session on open). A dropdown at the top of the drawer lets the user retrieve their recent past conversations — selecting one loads it as the active session in the drawer. New messages sent from the drawer are then part of that resumed conversation and appear in `/app/assistant` history.

- On drawer open: start a new chat session (call `POST /api/chats/new` or equivalent)
- Dropdown: populated from `GET /api/chats` (user's recent conversations — last N, e.g., 10)
- Selecting a past conversation from dropdown: load it (`GET /api/chats/{id}`) and resume in drawer
- "See full conversation →" link navigates to `/app/assistant` with the active chat ID pre-loaded
- New messages sent from the drawer appear in `/app/assistant` history in the same session
- History endpoint and auth token: per TKT-0060 amended spec

### 5. Stub mode (for Phase 2 / pre-Phase 6)

Per the ZO architecture doc Section 6.4 note for TKT-0016: the drawer should be build-time configurable as a stub — a greyed-out panel showing "ZO Intelligence — coming in Phase 6" — so Phase 2 shell work can include the drawer placeholder without requiring the ZO backend to be live.

Implement via a feature flag env var: `NEXT_PUBLIC_ZO_ENABLED=true|false`. When `false`, render the stub. When `true`, render the full drawer. Default to `false` until Phase 6 deployment.

### 6. Integration with platform shell layout

Wire the drawer into the root layout (`app/layout.tsx` or equivalent):
- `[ZO]` button in shell header/top-right
- `ZODrawer` component rendered at root level so it persists across page navigations
- Drawer z-index must sit above the main content area but below any modal dialogs

## Execution Notes

- **Do not start this ticket until TKT-0060 is DONE** — the API shape for the drawer depends on the Open WebUI adoption decision.
- **Do not start until TKT-0066 is DONE** — the backend must be running to test the drawer end-to-end.
- Read `zo-assistant-architecture.md` Section 2 in full before writing any component code.
- Phase 2 TKT-0016 agent: the stub mode (`NEXT_PUBLIC_ZO_ENABLED=false`) is what you should wire in Phase 2. Do not use a different approach.
- The drawer is a frontend-only ticket. Backend API changes (if any) belong in TKT-0045 (re-scoped).
- Source attribution rendering (citation tags, source drawer) is part of this ticket — ZO responses include `citations[]` from the backend; the drawer must render inline citation chips per the spec in zo-assistant-architecture.md Section 7.2.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] `ZODrawer` component renders in platform shell; togglable via `[ZO]` button and keyboard shortcut
- [ ] Context injection working: opening drawer on `/companies/[slug]` pre-populates company context and correct suggested chips
- [ ] Quick-action chips fire pre-formed payloads and render ZO response inline
- [ ] Conversation history loads from ZO backend; "See full history" links to `/app/assistant`
- [ ] New drawer messages appear in `/app/assistant` history in same session
- [ ] Stub mode (`NEXT_PUBLIC_ZO_ENABLED=false`) renders placeholder — no backend calls made
- [ ] Source attribution: citation chips render inline on ZO responses where `citations[]` is present
- [ ] Drawer persists open/closed state across Next.js route navigation

## Handoff Log
- 2026-04-08 | COO | Ticket created from session gap analysis. ZO drawer UI was not covered by TKT-0045 (backend), TKT-0046 (CLI), or any other existing ticket. Blocked on TKT-0060 (API shape) and TKT-0066 (backend running). Phase 6 implementation. TKT-0016 (Phase 2) should use stub mode only. | Status: BLOCKED | Next: activate when both TKT-0060 and TKT-0066 are DONE.

active.title: Build /app/assistant full-page React component in movecapital-platform (ZO chat UI)
active.created: Wed Apr 08 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Wed Apr 08 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context

TKT-0059 (Open WebUI evaluation, DONE 2026-04-08) confirmed that the ZO assistant UI will be built as a custom React component inside movecapital-platform — **not** as an embedded Open WebUI UI. Open WebUI runs as a headless backend sidecar. Two surfaces need React implementations: the full-page `/app/assistant` route and the ZO drawer (right panel — TKT-0067). This ticket covers the full-page surface only. TKT-0067 covers the drawer. PROG-001, WS-ASSISTANT, Phase 6.

This ticket was DISCOVERED during TKT-0059: the open-webui-zo-evaluation.md noted that no existing ticket covered the full-page assistant React component build.

## Current State

- Open WebUI evaluation complete: `01_Repos/zo/app/docs/design/open-webui-zo-evaluation.md`
- ZO architecture doc: `01_Repos/zo/app/docs/design/zo-assistant-architecture.md`
- movecapital-platform has an existing shallow `/app/assistant` route (placeholder — see TKT-0016 for context)
- Open WebUI will run as a Docker sidecar (TKT-0066) on an internal port (TBD — see OQ-C in eval doc)
- ZO Middleware (thin FastAPI) will sit between the React frontend and Open WebUI (TKT-0045 re-scoped by TKT-0060)
- TKT-0060 must complete first to confirm the final API shape of ZO Middleware before building this component

## Task

Build the `/app/assistant` full-page React component in movecapital-platform:

1. **Chat interface**: message input, message list (user + ZO messages), streaming response rendering
2. **Model selector**: dropdown populated from `GET /api/models` on ZO Middleware API
3. **Conversation history**: load/resume via `GET /api/chats` from ZO Middleware; sidebar list of past conversations
4. **Context injection**: on page load, read current platform state (active company, deal) from Next.js router or global store; inject as system context in the first message payload
5. **Source attribution rendering**: render `[Company data]`, `[Move research & analysis]` etc. inline citation tags as styled badges (see zo-assistant-architecture.md §7.2)
6. **Agent trigger chips**: quick-action chips for common ZO triggers ("Run comps", "Run analysis", "Search transcripts") that fire pre-formed payloads
7. **Long-running job status**: show spinner + job status for background analysis runs; toast on completion with link to result

## Execution Notes

- Read `open-webui-zo-evaluation.md` Section 2.3 (Option C architecture) and `zo-assistant-architecture.md` Sections 2.1, 2.2, 7.2 before starting.
- The component calls the **ZO Middleware API** (not Open WebUI directly). The Middleware API shape is defined by TKT-0060 amendment + TKT-0045 re-scoped build.
- Context injection is done by reading the Next.js router params and injecting a system message — see eval doc Section 2.3.
- Do not implement the ZO drawer in this ticket. The drawer is TKT-0067.
- Do not implement the ZO Middleware or Open WebUI deployment in this ticket — those are TKT-0045 and TKT-0066.
- Platform port is 3100. Open WebUI backend is internal only (not directly accessible from browser).
- Use the existing platform design system (colors, typography, component patterns) — do not introduce Open WebUI CSS.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `/app/assistant` route renders a functional chat interface calling ZO Middleware API
- [ ] Streaming responses render correctly
- [ ] Conversation history sidebar loads from `/api/chats`
- [ ] Context injection: company/deal context is passed when navigating from a company or deal page
- [ ] Source attribution citation tags render inline as styled badges
- [ ] Quick-action trigger chips visible and functional
- [ ] Long-running job status shown with spinner and toast completion
- [ ] Component matches platform design system (no Open WebUI CSS leakage)

## Handoff Log

- 2026-04-08 | Claude Code (COO session) | DISCOVERED during TKT-0059. Created ticket for the /app/assistant full-page React component — this work was not captured in any existing ticket. Blocked on TKT-0060 (ZO Middleware API shape must be finalised first) and TKT-0066 (Open WebUI must be running before the component can be tested end-to-end). | No implementation started. | Wait for TKT-0060 to close before starting. Read eval doc and ZO architecture doc first. | Do not confuse this with TKT-0067 (ZO drawer — right panel). These are parallel builds once unblocked.

active.title: Design ZO file upload integration — SharePoint source selection and deduplication guard
active.created: Wed Apr 08 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Wed Apr 08 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context

Open WebUI's file upload feature (attaches documents to ZO conversations for in-context retrieval) is included in the ZO feature set per Enzo's decision 2026-04-08. However, naively allowing drag-and-drop file upload creates two serious risks:
1. **Duplication**: a document already ingested into deal-rag (the canonical document store) would be ingested again into Open WebUI's local storage, creating two divergent copies with potentially different chunks.
2. **Out-of-band ingestion**: documents uploaded directly to Open WebUI bypass the platform's canonical ingest pipeline (deal-rag), creating orphan documents outside the team's shared knowledge base.

Enzo's requirement: "needs its own ticket because we would need to have it select documents from the shared SharePoint and to be sure we do not duplicate should a user drag and drop a document already in SharePoint, this is sensible not to make a mess."

This ticket designs the correct upload UX and deduplication strategy. PROG-001, WS-ASSISTANT, Phase 6.

## Current State

- deal-rag is the canonical document store for Move Capital deal documents (ACTIVE-LIVE on T418)
- No SharePoint connector exists yet in any service
- Open WebUI supports file upload to its own local/vector store (`/api/v1/files/`)
- The ZO Middleware layer (TKT-0045 re-scoped, TKT-0060) sits between the React frontend and Open WebUI — it is the right interception point for upload deduplication logic
- SharePoint is the team's shared document store — documents live there and are (or should be) ingested into deal-rag

## Task

Produce a design document at `01_Repos/zo/app/docs/design/zo-file-upload-design.md` covering:

### 1. Upload source model

Design two upload paths that ZO should support:

**Path A — SharePoint source picker**: User browses and selects a document from the shared Move Capital SharePoint. ZO checks if this document is already in deal-rag (by filename hash, SharePoint file ID, or document_id). If already ingested: ZO uses the existing deal-rag chunks directly (no re-upload). If not yet ingested: ZO triggers an ingest into deal-rag first, then uses the resulting chunks.

**Path B — Drag-and-drop / local file**: User drags a file from their machine. ZO must:
- Check if a document with the same filename or content hash already exists in deal-rag
- If duplicate: warn the user ("This document appears to already be in the platform — using the existing version") and use deal-rag chunks
- If not duplicate: ingest into deal-rag (canonical store) AND into the current ZO conversation context

**Key design constraint**: Open WebUI's local file store should NOT be used as a document store. All ingested documents must go through deal-rag. Open WebUI file upload is acceptable only as a temporary conversation attachment (ephemeral, not persisted to the knowledge base).

### 2. SharePoint connector design (scoping)

What is the minimum viable SharePoint integration needed for Path A?
- Microsoft Graph API connection (read-only scope: `Files.Read.All`)
- A file browser modal in the ZO chat UI: shows SharePoint document libraries, allows selection
- Returns: file name, SharePoint file ID, download URL (for ingest)

This is likely a new service or n8n workflow. Scope it clearly — is this a Phase 6 feature or Phase 7?

### 3. Deduplication strategy

Define the deduplication key for deal-rag documents:
- Option 1: Content hash (SHA-256 of file bytes) — robust but requires hashing on upload
- Option 2: SharePoint file ID (stable identifier from SharePoint) — only works for SharePoint-sourced files
- Option 3: Filename + file size — weak, collisions possible
- Recommend a composite key and describe how it is stored in deal-rag's vector metadata (extends `VectorChunkMetadata` from zo-assistant-architecture.md §7.3)

### 4. User experience spec

- What does the file picker modal look like?
- How is the duplicate warning surfaced?
- What feedback does the user get when a document is being ingested vs. already available?
- Can a user see which documents are "in the platform" vs. "uploaded just for this conversation"?

### 5. Deferred scope

What is NOT in scope for ZO V1 vs. what is deferred:
- Full SharePoint sync (all documents auto-ingested on a schedule) — likely Phase 7 or a separate ticket
- Per-user document visibility controls — deferred to per-user ZO phase

## Execution Notes

- Design only. No SharePoint connector implementation in this ticket.
- The deduplication check should be implemented in the ZO Middleware layer — it is in the best position to intercept upload requests and check against deal-rag.
- Microsoft Graph API access requires an app registration in the tenant's Azure AD — flag this as a dependency (Enzo must authorise the app registration).
- Read `zo-assistant-architecture.md` §7.3 for the `VectorChunkMetadata` schema — the dedup key field must be added there.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Two upload paths designed (SharePoint picker + drag-and-drop)
- [ ] SharePoint connector scoped (Phase 6 or deferred)
- [ ] Deduplication strategy defined with recommended composite key
- [ ] `VectorChunkMetadata` extension proposed (dedup key field)
- [ ] UX spec for duplicate warning and upload feedback
- [ ] Clear deferred scope list
- [ ] Design doc at `01_Repos/zo/app/docs/design/zo-file-upload-design.md`

## Handoff Log

- 2026-04-08 | COO | Ticket created from Enzo's ZO feature review (2026-04-08). File upload included in ZO feature set but flagged as requiring dedicated design: SharePoint source picker + deduplication guard. Canonical document store is deal-rag — Open WebUI's local store is not to be used as a persistent knowledge base. Blocked on TKT-0060 + TKT-0066 (same as other ZO Phase 6 build tickets). | Status: OPEN | Next: design doc first, SharePoint connector implementation is a follow-on ticket. | Azure AD app registration required for Microsoft Graph — flag to Enzo before implementation begins.

active.title: Design ZO knowledge base surface — expose platform RAG collections as named collections, no duplication
active.created: Wed Apr 08 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Wed Apr 08 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context

Open WebUI's Knowledge Base feature (create named collections, add files/URLs, attach to a model or conversation) is included in the ZO feature set per Enzo's decision 2026-04-08. However, Move Capital already has two live RAG services (deal-rag and transcript-rag-api) with substantial ingested content. Allowing users to create arbitrary Open WebUI knowledge bases would produce a third, fourth, fifth... vector store — fragmented, duplicated, unmanaged.

Enzo's requirement: "needs its own ticket because we would already have a lot of knowledge in the platform and we don't want to make a duplicated mess."

This ticket designs how ZO's knowledge base surface maps to the existing platform RAG services, ensuring users interact with a single coherent knowledge layer — not a proliferation of duplicate stores. PROG-001, WS-ASSISTANT, Phase 6.

## Current State

- `deal-rag` (ACTIVE-LIVE on T418): canonical store for deal documents, analyst memos, investment theses. REST API with `GET /search`, `GET /chunks/:id`.
- `transcript-rag-api` (ACTIVE-LIVE on T418): canonical store for call transcripts. REST API with `GET /search`, `GET /calls`.
- `podcast-rag-api`: not yet built (TKT-0028 design complete — Phase 6+). Will be a third RAG service.
- Open WebUI's built-in knowledge store: Chroma or pgvector, local to the Open WebUI container. Currently no data.
- VectorChunkMetadata schema defined in zo-assistant-architecture.md §7.3 — all RAG chunks must carry `source_type`, `source_name`, etc.

## Task

Produce a design document at `01_Repos/zo/app/docs/design/zo-knowledge-base-design.md` covering:

### 1. Named collection mapping

Open WebUI's knowledge base UI shows named collections (e.g., "Deal Documents", "Transcripts", "Podcasts"). These should map directly to the platform's existing RAG services — not be separate vector stores.

Design how each named collection maps:

| Collection name (ZO UI) | Backend service | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Deal Documents | deal-rag `GET /search` | All ingested deal docs, memos, theses |
| Call Transcripts | transcript-rag-api `GET /search` | All captured call transcripts |
| Podcast Knowledge | podcast-rag-api `GET /search` | Phase 6+ — not yet live |
| Web Research | deal-rag or new sub-collection | URL-ingested content (Feature N) |

**Key design constraint**: These collections must be read-only views into the existing services via the ZO Middleware — not copies. No data should be re-indexed into Open WebUI's local store for collections that already exist in deal-rag or transcript-rag-api.

### 2. Open WebUI knowledge API interception

Open WebUI exposes a knowledge base management API (`/api/v1/knowledge/`). When a user interacts with a named collection (search, attach to conversation), the ZO Middleware should intercept and route the request to the correct platform RAG service — returning results in Open WebUI's expected format.

Design the interception layer:
- Which Open WebUI knowledge API calls need to be intercepted?
- What format does Open WebUI expect for knowledge search results?
- How does the ZO Middleware translate between Open WebUI's format and deal-rag / transcript-rag-api response formats?

### 3. User-created collections (personal knowledge)

Should users be allowed to create their own personal knowledge collections (outside the three canonical ones)?

Design constraints:
- Personal collections are user-scoped (RLS enforced — no cross-user visibility)
- Personal collections may use Open WebUI's local store (acceptable for personal, user-managed content)
- Personal collections should NOT duplicate content from deal-rag or transcript-rag-api
- Admin can see collection metadata (names, sizes) but not content of personal collections unless they own them (per Enzo's K decision on memories)

Recommend: allow personal collections with a clear visual distinction from canonical platform collections (e.g., a "Personal" badge vs. "Platform" badge).

### 4. Ingestion path for new content

When a user adds a new document or URL to a collection:
- If adding to a canonical collection (Deal Documents, Transcripts): route ingest to deal-rag (this is the TKT-0071 upload flow)
- If adding to a personal collection: ingest locally in Open WebUI store (per §3 above)
- Never silently ingest to Open WebUI's store when the intent is platform-wide content

### 5. Collection visibility in conversations

When a user attaches a collection to a ZO conversation, design how the context window is populated:
- Canonical collections: ZO Middleware queries the relevant RAG service with the user's prompt; top-k chunks are returned and injected as context
- Personal collections: Open WebUI queries its local store; chunks injected directly

The user should see clearly which collections are attached to their active conversation.

### 6. Deferred scope

- Full admin collection management UI (Phase 7)
- Podcast Knowledge collection (depends on TKT-0028 implementation — Phase 6+)
- Cross-collection semantic search (unified search across deal-rag + transcript-rag simultaneously) — note as future work if not in V1

## Execution Notes

- Design only. No data migration or new ingestion in this ticket.
- The ZO Middleware interception layer design must be compatible with TKT-0045's re-scoped architecture (per TKT-0060 output).
- Read `zo-assistant-architecture.md` §7.3 (VectorChunkMetadata) and §6.1 (services ZO can call) before designing the interception layer.
- Do not assume Open WebUI's knowledge API format is stable — verify from the Open WebUI source (`backend/open_webui/routers/knowledge.py` in the open-webui repo).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Named collection → platform RAG service mapping designed
- [ ] Open WebUI knowledge API interception layer designed (format translation)
- [ ] Personal collections policy defined (allowed, user-scoped, no canonical duplication)
- [ ] Ingestion path designed (canonical vs. personal routing)
- [ ] Collection visibility in conversation context designed
- [ ] Deferred scope listed
- [ ] Design doc at `01_Repos/zo/app/docs/design/zo-knowledge-base-design.md`

## Handoff Log

- 2026-04-08 | COO | Ticket created from Enzo's ZO feature review (2026-04-08). Knowledge base included in ZO feature set but flagged as needing careful integration design: existing deal-rag and transcript-rag-api are the canonical stores; Open WebUI's local store must not duplicate them. ZO Middleware is the right interception point. Blocked on TKT-0060 + TKT-0066. | Status: OPEN | Next: design doc — read zo-assistant-architecture.md §6 and §7 first. | Personal collections in Open WebUI local store are acceptable; canonical collections must NOT be duplicated there.

active.title: Deploy Infisical vault to T418 gordon-stack and migrate existing env file secrets
active.created: Thu Apr 09 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Thu Apr 09 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context

TKT-0070 produced the secrets vault design decision document at `01_Repos/gordon-stack/docs/secrets-vault-design.md`. The selected tool is **Infisical (self-hosted)**. The selected env integration model is **Model A**: a deploy-time script reads secrets from the vault and writes `.env` files to `02_Runtime/gordon-prod/` before `docker compose up`. This ticket is the Phase 1 implementation: deploy Infisical to T418 as a gordon-stack sidecar and migrate all existing secrets from flat `.env` files.

This is a T418 infrastructure ticket (no programme, no workstream). It must be completed before TKT-0093 (per-user token middleware) can begin.

## Current State

- All secrets live in flat `.env` files under `02_Runtime/gordon-prod/` — no vault, no rotation, no audit log
- No Infisical instance exists on T418 or in gordon-stack
- gordon-stack compose file: `01_Repos/gordon-stack/docker-compose.yml`
- Design document: `01_Repos/gordon-stack/docs/secrets-vault-design.md`
- Secret categories defined in design doc Section 4; vault path scheme defined

## Task

1. **Add Infisical to gordon-stack compose**
   - Add three containers to `docker-compose.yml`: `infisical`, `infisical-db` (postgres:15-alpine), `infisical-redis` (redis:7-alpine)
   - Infisical on internal Docker network only — no external port unless behind nginx auth proxy
   - Named volumes for `infisical-db` and `infisical-redis` persistence
   - Use `.env.infisical` in `02_Runtime/gordon-prod/` for Infisical's own startup secrets (DB password, encryption key, JWT secret)

2. **Initial Infisical configuration**
   - Create organisation, projects, and environments matching gordon-stack service layout
   - Create machine identity (service token) for deploy automation role
   - Store machine identity token in `02_Runtime/gordon-prod/.env.infisical` (never in git)

3. **Secret migration**
   - Manually import all existing secrets from `.env` files into Infisical via web UI
   - Organise by vault path scheme from design doc Section 4 (`/infra/`, `/external-ai/`, `/external-data/`, etc.)
   - Do NOT print, echo, or include any secret values in any output, log, or file

4. **Write deploy script**
   - Create `03_Scripts/generate-envs.sh`
   - Script calls `infisical export` for each project/environment, writes to `02_Runtime/gordon-prod/`
   - Set output file permissions to `600`
   - Script must fail with non-zero exit if Infisical is unreachable (do not silently write empty files)

5. **Emergency fallback**
   - Before migrating, archive a timestamped copy of all existing `.env` files in `99_Archive/YYYY-MM-DD_pre-infisical-migration/` — directory listing only, NOT file contents in this ticket log
   - Document the fallback procedure: if Infisical is down, restore from archive `.env` files

6. **Enable audit log**
   - Confirm Infisical audit log is writing to a persistent volume
   - Note log path in `02_Runtime/gordon-prod/` or a named volume

7. **Update deployment runbook**
   - Update `01_Repos/gordon-stack/docs/operations.md` to include `generate-envs.sh` as a step before `docker compose up`

## Execution Notes

- gordon-stack repo is local-only (no GitHub remote). Commit changes but do not push.
- Do NOT print, echo, or include any `.env` contents, API keys, or secret values in any output or commit.
- Inspect currently running services before touching compose file. Copy first, verify second, cut over third.
- Infisical self-hosted requires a `ENCRYPTION_KEY` (128-bit hex), `AUTH_SECRET` (JWT signing key), and a `DB_CONNECTION_URI`. Generate these fresh — do not reuse existing secrets.
- Infisical web UI default port is 8080 — check for conflict with existing gordon-stack services before exposing.
- Risk: if `generate-envs.sh` fails silently, a compose restart would use stale `.env` files. The script must exit non-zero on vault unreachability.
- After migration, verify all services start correctly with vault-generated `.env` files before decommissioning the direct `.env` edit workflow.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Infisical running as gordon-stack sidecar (docker compose ps shows it healthy)
- [ ] All existing secrets migrated to Infisical (keys present in vault; original `.env` files archived)
- [ ] `03_Scripts/generate-envs.sh` generates correct `.env` files for all service groups
- [ ] Generated `.env` files have `600` permissions
- [ ] All gordon-stack services start successfully from vault-generated `.env` files
- [ ] Audit log active and writing to persistent volume
- [ ] `01_Repos/gordon-stack/docs/operations.md` updated with new deploy workflow
- [ ] Emergency fallback `.env` archive in `99_Archive/`
- [ ] gordon-stack compose change committed (local only — no remote)

## Handoff Log

- 2026-04-09 | Security Engineer + Backend Architect | Ticket created from TKT-0070 design decision. Infisical selected as vault tool, Model A env integration selected. This ticket is the Phase 1 deployment implementation. No blockers — startable immediately. | Status: OPEN | Next: add Infisical to gordon-stack compose, configure, migrate secrets, write generate-envs.sh. | Do not expose any secret values. Archive existing .env files before migration begins.
- 2026-04-09 | COO | Ticket added to index per TKT-0070 vault design outcome.
- 2026-04-10 | COO | Agent work complete. Compose entries (infisical, infisical-postgres, infisical-redis), .env.infisical placeholder, and generate-envs.sh all written. Status updated to WAITING_INPUT. Enzo must: (1) `docker compose up -d infisical infisical-postgres infisical-redis`, (2) complete Infisical web UI setup, (3) manually migrate secrets from flat .env files, (4) verify generate-envs.sh produces correct output, (5) confirm all services start from vault-generated envs. Also outstanding: create archive of .env files in 99_Archive/ before migration. | Blocked on Enzo bringing up the Infisical instance and migrating secrets. | Do not print secret values. Infisical is on port 8086 — no external port exposure without nginx auth proxy.
- 2026-04-09 | Security Engineer | Executed TKT-0070 Phase 1 staging. `docker-compose.yml` updated with `infisical` (port 8086), `infisical-postgres`, and `infisical-redis`. `02_Runtime/gordon-prod/.env.infisical` placeholder created. `03_Scripts/generate-envs.sh` wrapper created. Secrets not yet migrated. Awaiting manual bring-up and secret transfer. | Status: OPEN (waiting on Enzo for live cutover).

active.title: Implement per-user token auth middleware for deal-rag and transcript-rag-api
active.created: Thu Apr 09 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Thu Apr 09 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context

TKT-0070 designed the T418 central secrets vault and the per-user token lifecycle. TKT-0092 deploys Infisical (Phase 1). This ticket is Phase 2: implement the auth middleware in deal-rag and transcript-rag-api, create the `user_tokens` Supabase table, implement the token provisioning endpoint in ZO Middleware, and replace shared API keys with per-user bearer tokens for user-facing endpoints.

The immediate business driver: ZO Middleware and the RAG APIs need per-user auth tokens (one token per platform user per service) rather than shared API keys. This was raised during TKT-0059 ("we would need to have dedicated auth for each user"). Without this, user isolation, per-user audit trails, and token revocation are impossible.

This ticket depends on TKT-0092 (Infisical vault deployed) and should be read alongside the design document at `01_Repos/gordon-stack/docs/secrets-vault-design.md` (Sections 3 and 5.3 Phase 2).

## Current State

- deal-rag and transcript-rag-api use shared API keys for all callers — no per-user auth
- No `user_tokens` table in Supabase
- No token provisioning logic in ZO Middleware
- Infisical vault: not yet deployed (TKT-0092 prerequisite)
- Auth services: Supabase Auth live (TKT-0036 DONE), Keycloak TKT-0042 OPEN, GoTrue OIDC TKT-0053 OPEN
- Design document: `01_Repos/gordon-stack/docs/secrets-vault-design.md`

## Task

### 1. Supabase migration — `user_tokens` table

Create a Supabase migration that adds the `user_tokens` table (schema in design doc Section 3.3):

```sql
create table user_tokens (
  id          uuid primary key default gen_random_uuid(),
  user_id     uuid not null references auth.users(id) on delete cascade,
  service     text not null,  -- 'deal-rag' | 'transcript-rag-api' | 'zo-middleware'
  token_hash  text not null,  -- SHA-256 hex of plaintext token
  scopes      text[] not null default '{}',
  issued_at   timestamptz not null default now(),
  expires_at  timestamptz,
  revoked_at  timestamptz,
  constraint user_tokens_user_service_unique unique (user_id, service)
);

create index on user_tokens(token_hash);
create index on user_tokens(user_id, service);
```

Apply RLS:
- Authenticated users can `SELECT` their own rows (`user_id = auth.uid()`)
- No `INSERT`, `UPDATE`, `DELETE` from client — service role only
- Service role key used by provisioning endpoint and middleware lookup

### 2. Token provisioning endpoint (ZO Middleware or dedicated token-service)

Implement `POST /tokens/provision` in ZO Middleware (or as a standalone `token-service` FastAPI container):

- Requires valid Keycloak/GoTrue JWT in `Authorization: Bearer` header
- Extracts `sub` (Keycloak user ID) → resolves to `auth.users.id` in Supabase
- Generates a cryptographically random 256-bit bearer token
- SHA-256 hashes the token
- Inserts row into `user_tokens` for each requested service with default scopes
- Stores token metadata in Infisical at `/users/{keycloak_id}/tokens/{service}` using the Infisical machine identity token
- Returns plaintext token to caller **once only** — not re-readable after issuance

Trigger: called at platform onboarding (first login). ZO Middleware first-login hook should call this endpoint.

### 3. Auth middleware — deal-rag

Add FastAPI dependency `verify_user_token` to deal-rag query endpoints:

1. Extract `Authorization: Bearer <token>` from request headers
2. SHA-256 hash the token
3. Query `user_tokens` where `token_hash = <hash>` AND `service = 'deal-rag'` AND `revoked_at IS NULL` AND (`expires_at IS NULL` OR `expires_at > now()`)
4. If no row found: return HTTP 401
5. Check that required scope is in `scopes` array; if not: return HTTP 403
6. Attach `user_id` to request state for downstream use

Apply `verify_user_token` to: `/query`, `/search` endpoints (scope: `deal-rag:read`).  
Apply elevated check to: `/ingest`, `/upload` endpoints (scope: `deal-rag:ingest`) — these should reject tokens without the elevated scope.

### 4. Auth middleware — transcript-rag-api

Same pattern as deal-rag with `service = 'transcript-rag-api'`:

- `/query`, `/search` → scope `transcript-rag:read`
- `/ingest`, `/upload` → scope `transcript-rag:ingest`

### 5. Token rotation hook

Implement rotation logic in the provisioning service:
- On rotation request: generate new token, insert new `user_tokens` row, set `revoked_at = now()` on old row
- Return new plaintext token to authenticated caller
- Update Infisical secret at `/users/{keycloak_id}/tokens/{service}`

### 6. Revocation procedure documentation

Document emergency revocation procedure in `01_Repos/gordon-stack/docs/operations.md`:
```sql
-- Revoke a user's token for a specific service
UPDATE user_tokens
SET revoked_at = now()
WHERE user_id = '<uuid>' AND service = '<service_name>';
```
This takes effect on the next request — no service restart required.

### 7. Integration test

Write integration tests covering:
- Valid token → 200 on deal-rag query endpoint
- Revoked token → 401
- Expired token → 401
- Wrong service token → 401
- Missing scope → 403

## Execution Notes

- This ticket depends on TKT-0092 (Infisical deployed) — do not start auth middleware until vault is running and `user_tokens` table is in place.
- Auth middleware must handle the pre-TKT-0053 case (Supabase auth.users.id used as user identifier) AND the post-TKT-0053 case (Keycloak sub mapped to auth.users.id via GoTrue OIDC). TKT-0053 may not be complete when this ticket is worked.
- Do not disable or bypass existing service auth mechanisms until the new middleware is verified in staging/test.
- The Supabase service role key used by auth middleware must be stored in Infisical (not hardcoded) and injected via Model A env generation from TKT-0092.
- deal-rag repo: `01_Repos/deal-rag`; transcript-rag-api repo: `01_Repos/transcript-rag-api`. Both are automation-writing repos (n8n-git-sync authoring pattern) — check git flow policy before committing.
- Commit identity: Nassau-1 / enzoterrier@gmail.com. Message format: `<type>: <description> — TKT-0093`. Push to remote after each commit.
- Run `git diff --ignore-cr-at-eol` before each commit to avoid CRLF-only commits.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `user_tokens` table created in Supabase with RLS applied
- [ ] Token provisioning endpoint implemented and tested (`POST /tokens/provision`)
- [ ] Platform onboarding flow calls provisioning endpoint on first login
- [ ] Auth middleware deployed in deal-rag (all user-facing endpoints protected)
- [ ] Auth middleware deployed in transcript-rag-api (all user-facing endpoints protected)
- [ ] Revoked token returns HTTP 401 within one request cycle
- [ ] Expired token returns HTTP 401
- [ ] Wrong-scope token returns HTTP 403
- [ ] Token metadata stored in Infisical at correct path
- [ ] Revocation procedure documented in operations.md
- [ ] Integration tests written and passing
- [ ] Shared API keys for user-facing endpoints retired (or gated behind admin scope)

## Handoff Log

- 2026-04-09 | Security Engineer + Backend Architect | Ticket created from TKT-0070 design decision (Section 3 per-user token lifecycle, Section 5.3 Phase 2). Blocked on TKT-0092 (Infisical deployment). Design spec is complete — implementation can be planned but not executed until vault is running. Auth middleware must handle both pre- and post-TKT-0053 identity lookup. | Status: OPEN | Next: wait for TKT-0092 DONE, then create Supabase migration, implement provisioning endpoint, add middleware to deal-rag and transcript-rag-api. | Do not retire shared keys until new middleware is verified end-to-end.

active.title: Populate Infisical vault with live T418 secrets and verify generate-envs.sh

# Scope

The Infisical secrets vault infrastructure has been staged on T418 (TKT-0092). The containers (`infisical`, `infisical-postgres`, `infisical-redis`) are configured and the bootstrap keys have been automatically generated.

This ticket requires a human operator (Enzo) to perform the manual mapping of live T418 `.env` secrets into the new vault. Agents are strictly prohibited from reading or moving the live `.env` contents.

## Human Action Items (Enzo)

1. Open your browser and navigate to `http://localhost:8086`.
2. Create the first "Root Admin" account (you will need to choose a master password).
3. In the Infisical Web UI, create an organization and a project (e.g., `gordon-stack`).
4. Read your existing `.env` files from `02_Runtime/gordon-prod/` and manually copy/paste their contents into the Infisical project environment (e.g., `production`).
5. (Optional but recommended) Run `03_Scripts/generate-envs.sh` and verify it restores the proper `.env` outputs from the vault.

## Success Criteria

- Root admin account is active.
- Existing platform API keys and database passwords reside securely within Infisical.
- Agent routing can transparently depend on the vault in future tasks (e.g., TKT-0093 token provisioning).

## Handoff Log
