active.title: Infrastructure node strategy — decision record, BoM shape, failure-domain plan
active.created: Thu Apr 16 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Thu Apr 16 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context

T418 currently hosts the entire `gordon-stack` (16+ services across 6 networks): Nginx, platform, n8n, Keycloak, RAG APIs, Supabase/Postgres+pgvector, Neo4j, Redis, Infisical, observability, Open WebUI, Cloudflared. Observability monitors the host it runs on; secrets share blast radius with app tier; Postgres and app services share disk and RAM. T418 is also scheduled for decommission. The binding constraint is failure domain, not compute. A decision record is required before hardware is procured so that every downstream ticket (procurement, node standup, backups, relocations) references a single agreed architecture.

## Current State

- One production node: **T418** — hosts gordon-stack per `programme_001_architecture.md.resolved` §2.
- One AI runtime: **Mac Mini M4 (64 GB)** — Ollama + Gemma 4 27B Q4/Q8 for 16 local-eligible agents (TKT-0134).
- Network segmentation in progress: net-frontend, net-backend, net-data, net-assistant, net-secrets, net-observability (TKT-0098, TKT-0109).
- Existing infra backlog implicitly assumes single-node T418: TKT-0063 (Uptime Kuma), TKT-0064 (Loki), TKT-0065 (Prometheus), TKT-0092 (Infisical), TKT-0095 (Postgres consolidation to reclaim RAM). These need re-homing once the strategy lands.
- No NAS. No verified restore drill. No second failure domain.

## Task

Produce `00_Standards/infrastructure-node-strategy.md` capturing:

1. **Node taxonomy.** Four categories: tower-class x86 (state/DB tier), SFF/tiny x86 (app tier), refurb tiny x86 (control plane), Apple Silicon appliance (AI). No Raspberry Pi (ARM portability risk; refurb tiny x86 is same price and x86-native). No GPU node now.
2. **Role-to-node mapping.** Which services live on which node class. Postgres/pgvector + Neo4j + Infisical → tower-class (Node-2). App tier (platform, APIs, n8n, Keycloak, nginx, Cloudflared, Open WebUI, Redis) → SFF (Node-1). External probes + DNS + bastion + staging sandbox → refurb tiny (Node-3). Ollama + faster-whisper → Mac Mini (appliance, out of cluster).
3. **Horizon plan.**
   - Horizon A (0–3 months): 3 × x86 nodes + Mac Mini + NAS + UPS.
   - Horizon B (3–12 months, trigger-gated): promote Postgres to its own box if RAM/IO pressure sustained.
   - Horizon C (12+ months, trigger-gated): Mac Studio upgrade OR GPU drop-in on tower node — decided when a workload pulls, not pre-committed.
4. **Failure-domain rules.** Observability must not run on the host it monitors. Secrets must not share blast radius with app tier. Postgres is the crown jewel; first to isolate when load permits. NAS is backup-only, never a service host.
5. **Rejected options** (documented so they are not re-opened): Pi for anything load-bearing, single-big-tower consolidation, GPU-now, CPU-inference on production host, ARM for app services, skipping NAS.
6. **Decision log entry** in `PROGRAMME-001-STATUS.md` "Key decisions locked" section.

## Execution Notes

- This ticket produces a document and a decision lock. No hardware procurement yet (that is TKT-0136).
- Reference the analysis in this session's chat transcript and the inputs it read: `PROGRAMME-001-STATUS.md`, `TICKETS.md`, `programme_001_architecture.md.resolved`, `implementation_plan.md.resolved` v3.
- Keep the document decision-grade, not marketing-grade. Every rejected option must have one sentence of reasoning so future-you doesn't re-open it.
- The standards doc belongs in `00_Standards/` per governance layout. Sync to `01_Repos/Codex/` at end of session per CLAUDE.md workspace governance rule (TKT-0091).
- Do NOT modify existing TKT-0063/0064/0065/0092 — their relocations are captured in TKT-0138/0139 which block-chain from this one.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `00_Standards/infrastructure-node-strategy.md` exists and covers taxonomy, role mapping, Horizon A/B/C, failure-domain rules, rejected options.
- [ ] `PROGRAMME-001-STATUS.md` "Key decisions locked" includes the node strategy decision with date and ticket reference.
- [ ] Six successor tickets (TKT-0136 through TKT-0140 + this one) exist and cross-reference via `blocks`/`blocked_by`.
- [ ] Document committed and pushed to `01_Repos/Codex/` mirror.

## Handoff Log

- 2026-04-16 | Claude (Opus 4.6) | Ticket created alongside strategy session. Buying list drafted at `C:\Users\LENOVO\Desktop\AI Dump Folder\2026.04.16\hardware-buying-list-2026-04-16.md`. Successor tickets 0136–0140 created in same session. Status doc updated with decision lock. | Next: author the formal `00_Standards/infrastructure-node-strategy.md` document, then unblock TKT-0136 for procurement. | Pitfall: do not let "bigger T418" or "Pi witness" sneak back in during procurement — both are explicitly rejected.

active.title: Hardware procurement — buying list, sourcing, acceptance testing
active.created: Thu Apr 16 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Thu Apr 16 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context

The node strategy locked in TKT-0135 requires three x86 nodes (tower-class + SFF + refurb tiny), a NAS, and a UPS. The Mac Mini M4 is already on-prem. Procurement is an Enzo action — agents cannot purchase. This ticket holds the buying list, vendor/source preferences, acceptance criteria, and the receive-and-burn-in protocol so that hardware arrives in a known-good state before any node standup ticket begins.

## Current State

- Buying list drafted: `C:\Users\LENOVO\Desktop\AI Dump Folder\2026.04.16\hardware-buying-list-2026-04-16.md`.
- T418 still in service. Mac Mini M4 operational.
- No NAS, no UPS, no second failure domain.

## Task

1. **Enzo reviews the buying list** at `C:\Users\LENOVO\Desktop\AI Dump Folder\2026.04.16\hardware-buying-list-2026-04-16.md` and confirms target units, budget, and sourcing (refurb marketplaces, second-hand from known sellers, vs new retail).
2. **Source and purchase** the following (see buying list for specs):
   - 1 × tower-class / SFF workstation (DB/state node). Target: Dell Precision T3xxx / T5xxx, HP Z2/Z4, Lenovo ThinkStation P3xx or equivalent. 32–64 GB ECC-capable, 2× NVMe bays, full-length PCIe slot, standard ATX/SFX PSU.
   - 1 × SFF/tiny (app tier node). Target: Lenovo M720q/M920q/M90q, HP EliteDesk 800 G5/G6 Mini, Dell OptiPlex 7070/7090 Micro. 32–64 GB, 1 TB+ NVMe.
   - 1 × refurb tiny (control plane). Target: same family as above but prior generation. 8–16 GB, 256 GB+ NVMe.
   - 1 × NAS: 2-bay minimum (Synology DS224+/DS423+ or equivalent self-built ZFS mini) with 2× 4–8 TB drives for mirrored setup.
   - 1 × UPS: sized for all 3 x86 + Mac Mini + NAS under full load (~400–600 VA headroom). USB/serial monitoring supported for signal-based shutdown.
   - Cables, 1 GbE switch if current one is saturated, rails/mounting if needed.
3. **Receive and burn-in.** For each x86 unit on arrival:
   - Memtest86+ pass (≥1 full pass).
   - smartctl self-test on NVMe.
   - Fresh Debian 12 or Ubuntu 24.04 LTS install (OS decision per TKT-0135 standards doc).
   - 24-hour idle burn-in + `stress-ng` 2-hour CPU/RAM soak.
   - Record serial numbers, purchase date, source, price, warranty terms in `02_Runtime/inventory.md`.
4. **NAS burn-in.** RAID-1 / ZFS mirror setup, first scrub, first snapshot test, verify SMB + SSH access.
5. **UPS burn-in.** Battery self-test, pull-the-plug test with no load then with light load, confirm signal reaches Node-2 for ordered shutdown.

## Execution Notes

- Prefer refurb business-class units from reputable sellers (Bargain Hardware, ServerMonkey, local IT refurbishers) over consumer-grade or unbranded. Business refurb comes with drivers, UEFI updates, and is built for 5+ year duty cycles.
- Do NOT buy cheapest-available gaming PCs. Thermals, noise, PSU quality, and office acceptability matter.
- ECC is nice-to-have on the DB node, not a hard requirement — if the right refurb unit appears without ECC at a big discount, take it.
- Mac Mini already owned — do NOT buy a second.
- Do NOT buy GPU hardware in this round. Horizon C decision, not now.
- Do NOT buy Raspberry Pi or any ARM SBC. Strategy rejected that path.
- Defer managed switch, 10 GbE, rack gear — not needed at this scale.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Buying list reviewed and approved by Enzo; any spec deviations captured in handoff.
- [ ] All units purchased and received.
- [ ] Burn-in complete for each unit; records in `02_Runtime/inventory.md`.
- [ ] NAS RAID/ZFS mirror operational and scrubbed.
- [ ] UPS signal wiring verified end-to-end.
- [ ] Successor tickets TKT-0137/0138/0139/0140 unblocked.

## Handoff Log

- 2026-04-16 | Claude (Opus 4.6) | Created as gating ticket for procurement. Buying list written to `C:\Users\LENOVO\Desktop\AI Dump Folder\2026.04.16\hardware-buying-list-2026-04-16.md`. Status set to WAITING_INPUT pending Enzo review. | Next: Enzo to review buying list, source units, purchase, and run burn-in. | Pitfall: strategy rejected Pi, GPU-now, and consolidation-into-one-big-box — resist vendor upsell into any of these.

active.title: Node-1 standup (app tier) + T418 → Node-1 cutover runbook
active.created: Thu Apr 16 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Thu Apr 16 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context

Node-1 is the SFF/tiny x86 that replaces T418 as the production app tier. It runs nginx, platform, n8n, Keycloak, Redis, and all FastAPI services (deal-rag, transcript-rag-api, fundraise-intel, comps-pipeline, deal-analysis-api, ZO Middleware), Open WebUI, Cloudflared. It does NOT run Postgres, Neo4j, Infisical, or observability — those belong on Node-2 (TKT-0138) and Node-3 (TKT-0139). This ticket covers Node-1 standup and the T418-to-Node-1 cutover. Cutover only begins after Node-2 (DB tier) is live and backup/restore is verified (TKT-0140), so Postgres is safe before the app tier migrates.

## Current State

- T418: production, running everything.
- Node-1: procured and burned in (TKT-0136 done).
- Node-2: state tier live with Postgres primary + Neo4j + Infisical (TKT-0138 done).
- Node-3: control plane live (TKT-0139 done).
- NAS: backups running, restore drill passed (TKT-0140 done).

## Task

1. **Base OS + hardening.** Debian 12 or Ubuntu 24.04 LTS. SSH-key-only, UFW, automatic security updates, Docker Engine + compose plugin, Tailscale/WireGuard to join internal admin mesh. Reference `00_Standards/runtime-machine-standard.md`.
2. **Clone gordon-stack.** `01_Repos/gordon-stack` checked out. `.env` material pulled from Infisical on Node-2 (not from T418 files — cutover opportunity to clean secrets).
3. **Compose changes.** Update service definitions so that:
   - Postgres-dependent services (Keycloak, n8n, Open WebUI, all APIs) point to Node-2 Postgres over the internal network.
   - Neo4j consumers (deal-analysis-api) point to Node-2 Neo4j.
   - Infisical SDK config points to Node-2 Infisical instance.
   - Prometheus/Loki scrape targets registered with Node-2 observability stack.
   - Uptime Kuma on Node-3 probes Node-1 externally.
4. **Side-by-side verification.** Start Node-1 stack with T418 still live. Point staging DNS to Node-1. Run smoke tests: login flow, platform page render, deal-rag query, transcript-rag query, n8n workflow execute, Cloudflared tunnel health, Keycloak realm import.
5. **Cutover window.** Scheduled with Enzo. Drain T418 traffic (stop services), final Postgres dump to NAS (belt + braces over Node-2 replica), DNS flip via Cloudflare, re-verify smoke tests on Node-1 as primary.
6. **Freeze T418.** Keep powered, services stopped, as cold rollback for ≥2 weeks. Do not wipe. Document exact command to re-enable if rollback needed.
7. **Post-cutover.** Update `runtime-layout.md`, `02_Runtime/inventory.md`, and every doc referencing T418 as primary.

## Execution Notes

- This is a risky runtime change. Use `00_Standards/runtime-change-control-checklist.md`.
- Do NOT cut over until all preconditions are green: Node-2 DB stable, NAS restore drill documented, Node-3 probing externally.
- Do NOT copy `.env` files from T418 to Node-1 directly. Pull from Infisical. This is the one-time cleanup that removes stale secrets.
- Do NOT delete T418 state during this ticket. Wipe is a later decision and requires its own ticket.
- Do NOT modify Mac Mini or Ollama config — the appliance endpoint stays at the same hostname, only DB/service endpoints change.
- CRLF check: `git diff --ignore-cr-at-eol` on any gordon-stack commits before pushing (per `00_Standards/git-flow-policy.md`).
- gordon-stack is a local-only repo — no push, only local commits with clear messages.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Node-1 running production gordon-stack, all services green, all healthchecks passing.
- [ ] All Postgres/Neo4j/Infisical traffic routed to Node-2 (verified via `docker exec` + connection test).
- [ ] DNS and Cloudflared tunnel pointing to Node-1.
- [ ] Smoke test suite (login, platform, RAG queries, n8n workflow, webhook receipt) passes end-to-end on Node-1.
- [ ] T418 powered on but all services stopped; rollback command documented in ticket.
- [ ] `runtime-layout.md` and `02_Runtime/inventory.md` updated.
- [ ] Handoff Log closes with an explicit `runtime verified` state, not just `deployed`.

## Handoff Log

- 2026-04-16 | Claude (Opus 4.6) | Ticket created. Blocked on TKT-0136, 0138, 0140 per cutover preconditions. | Next: wait for predecessors. When unblocked, author the detailed cutover runbook as a sub-doc, schedule window with Enzo. | Pitfall: do not skip the side-by-side verification phase; do not wipe T418 until the 2-week rollback window closes.

active.title: Node-2 standup (state/DB tier) — Postgres, Neo4j, Infisical relocation
active.created: Thu Apr 16 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Thu Apr 16 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context

Node-2 is the tower-class / SFF workstation that hosts all stateful, failure-sensitive services: Supabase (Postgres + pgvector + GoTrue), Neo4j + Graphiti, Infisical, and the observability storage tier (Prometheus TSDB, Loki, Grafana). Separating state from the app tier is the core failure-domain decision from TKT-0135: observability must not run on the host it monitors, secrets must not share blast radius with app services, and Postgres gets dedicated IO/RAM headroom. This node is also the GPU-upgrade path for Horizon C — it must have a full-length PCIe slot and an adequately sized PSU.

## Current State

- T418 hosts all of the above. Single failure domain.
- TKT-0063 (Uptime Kuma), TKT-0064 (Loki+Promtail+Grafana), TKT-0065 (Prometheus+cAdvisor+node_exporter) all WAITING_INPUT — compose written but not brought up. These will land on Node-2 directly, skipping T418 entirely.
- TKT-0092 (Infisical) WAITING_INPUT — deploys on Node-2.
- TKT-0095 (consolidate n8n + Infisical Postgres into Supabase) — executes after Node-2 Postgres is primary.
- Node-2 procured and burned in (TKT-0136 done). NAS backup target ready (TKT-0140 done).

## Task

1. **Base OS + hardening.** Debian 12 or Ubuntu 24.04 LTS, same hardening baseline as Node-1. Docker Engine + compose, Tailscale mesh, ZFS root optional if the box supports it.
2. **Disk layout.** 2× NVMe: one dedicated to Postgres data volume, one for everything else (Neo4j, Infisical, Prom/Loki TSDB, Docker volumes). Filesystem tuning for Postgres per PostgreSQL docs (noatime, appropriate block size).
3. **Postgres primary standup.** Stand up Supabase on Node-2 as a fresh instance. Restore the latest pg dump from NAS (produced by TKT-0140 backup discipline). Verify all schemas present: canonical (company_identity, deal_identity, person_identity), shadow_*, pgvector embeddings, Keycloak realm, n8n workflows, Open WebUI, agent_tasks (if created), wiki tables.
4. **Neo4j standup.** Fresh Neo4j + Graphiti on Node-2. Restore from latest graph export; if none, defer re-ingest to a separate ticket and stand up empty.
5. **Infisical standup.** Fresh Infisical on Node-2. Restore sealed export from NAS. Re-seal with fresh root key. Update root key share custody (one on-site, one off-site, one in password manager).
6. **Observability stack.** Bring up Prometheus + cAdvisor + node_exporter + Grafana + Loki + Promtail on Node-2. Configure scrape targets: Node-1 (once live), Node-2 self, Node-3, Mac Mini (node_exporter via Tailscale), T418 (until retired).
7. **Network segmentation.** Honour the net-frontend / net-backend / net-data / net-secrets / net-observability boundaries from TKT-0098. Cross-node traffic via a dedicated internal interface or VLAN; avoid exposing Postgres/Neo4j/Infisical on the LAN uplink.
8. **Replication hook (deferred).** Prepare Postgres for logical replication so a future read-replica (or Horizon B split) is a config change, not a rebuild.
9. **Resource limits.** Apply TKT-0099 memory/swap/PID/CPU limits. Apply TKT-0100 cap_drop + non-root + read-only rootfs where service permits. Apply TKT-0106 chown fix for Postgres host dir non-root execution.

## Execution Notes

- Restore FROM NAS, not from T418 live volumes. Forces the backup/restore path to be exercised (this is a feature).
- Do NOT run Postgres and the full observability stack without checking cumulative RAM fits with headroom. If tight, split observability to Node-3 — but only if Node-3 has the RAM.
- Do NOT expose Postgres, Neo4j, or Infisical to the LAN uplink. Internal mesh or Docker network only. Verify with `ss -tlnp` after bring-up.
- Do NOT skip the Infisical root-key re-sealing step. Restoring and reusing the old root key perpetuates the shared-blast-radius problem this ticket exists to fix.
- Do NOT start Node-1 cutover (TKT-0137) until Node-2 smoke tests pass and a round-trip NAS restore has been demonstrated on Node-2 hardware.
- CRLF check on any gordon-stack compose edits before push (local-only repo still applies).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Node-2 running Postgres primary, Neo4j, Infisical, full observability stack.
- [ ] All schemas restored and verified (row counts match T418 within expected delta).
- [ ] Internal-only network binding verified for PG/Neo4j/Infisical (no LAN exposure).
- [ ] Infisical re-sealed with fresh root key; share custody documented.
- [ ] Prometheus scraping at least Node-2 self and T418 (interim); dashboards load in Grafana.
- [ ] Resource limits (TKT-0099), cap_drop (TKT-0100), chown fix (TKT-0106) applied where relevant.
- [ ] `02_Runtime/inventory.md` updated with Node-2 serial, role, disk layout, RAM.
- [ ] Handoff Log closes with explicit `runtime verified` state and the observed RAM/CPU headroom numbers.

## Handoff Log

- 2026-04-16 | Claude (Opus 4.6) | Ticket created. Relocates TKT-0063/0064/0065/0092 to Node-2 by construction. | Next: wait for TKT-0136 (hardware) and TKT-0140 (NAS + backup). When unblocked, execute in the order above. | Pitfall: Infisical root key re-sealing is easy to skip and undermines the entire failure-domain split; treat it as mandatory.

active.title: Node-3 standup (control plane) — external probes, DNS, bastion, staging sandbox
active.created: Thu Apr 16 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Thu Apr 16 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context

Node-3 is the refurb tiny x86 that replaces the Pi-shaped role rejected in TKT-0135. It runs only control-plane workloads — things that must be alive when the main stack is down: an external Uptime Kuma (probing Node-1 and Node-2 from outside their failure domain), DNS (Pi-hole or Unbound), a second Cloudflared tunnel for admin surfaces, a Tailscale/WireGuard entry point, and the offline recovery key holder for Infisical. When idle (most of the time), it doubles as a staging sandbox for Twenty/Vexa (TKT-0008, TKT-0009) and any non-prod experimentation.

## Current State

- No out-of-band monitoring exists. Uptime Kuma (TKT-0063) planned for T418, which is the anti-pattern this ticket fixes.
- No external DNS. No second tunnel.
- Node-3 procured and burned in (TKT-0136 done).

## Task

1. **Base OS + hardening.** Debian 12 or Ubuntu 24.04 LTS. Minimal install. Same hardening baseline as Node-1/Node-2 but lighter on services.
2. **External Uptime Kuma.** Deploy Uptime Kuma on Node-3 (separate instance from any future one on Node-2 — this one's value is being *outside* the monitored cluster). Probe targets: Node-1 platform URL, Node-2 Grafana URL, Cloudflare tunnel endpoints, Mac Mini Ollama `:11434`. Telegram alerting wired to dedicated bot token (not the main n8n bot — separate failure path).
3. **DNS.** Pi-hole or Unbound. Serve LAN names (node1, node2, node3, macmini, nas) + ad/tracker blocking for office network. Upstream to 1.1.1.1 / 9.9.9.9 with DoT.
4. **Tailscale/WireGuard entry.** Configure as a stable mesh entry point so Enzo/admin access can always reach the environment even during a Node-1 reboot.
5. **Second Cloudflared tunnel (admin-only).** Separate tunnel for Grafana, Uptime Kuma UI, and any break-glass admin surfaces. Zero Trust access rules tight.
6. **Offline recovery key custody.** Store one share of the Infisical root key split (produced in TKT-0138) on Node-3 in a passphrase-protected file. Document the other two share locations (password manager, off-site) without storing them.
7. **Staging sandbox (optional, when idle).** Separate Docker Compose project for Twenty/Vexa sandbox experimentation. Never shares data with production. Resource-limited so it cannot starve the control-plane workloads.

## Execution Notes

- Resist the temptation to put observability storage on Node-3. Prometheus/Loki belong on Node-2 (TKT-0138) where they have the disk and RAM. Node-3 runs the *external probe*, not the *storage*.
- Do NOT route production Cloudflare traffic through Node-3. Production tunnel stays on Node-1. The Node-3 tunnel is admin-only with Zero Trust auth.
- Do NOT put load-bearing production services here. Every rejected Pi-role from TKT-0135 lives here; nothing else.
- Verify the external probes actually fail when Node-1/Node-2 are down. Do a controlled test: stop nginx on Node-1, confirm Telegram alert arrives within SLO.
- Keep the staging sandbox on a separate Docker network from anything touching production paths.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Node-3 running Uptime Kuma, DNS, Tailscale, second Cloudflared tunnel.
- [ ] Uptime Kuma probes Node-1, Node-2, Mac Mini; alerts fire on simulated outage.
- [ ] DNS resolving LAN names on all office devices.
- [ ] Second Cloudflared tunnel up with Zero Trust rules for admin surfaces.
- [ ] Infisical recovery key share stored encrypted on Node-3; custody documented.
- [ ] `02_Runtime/inventory.md` updated with Node-3 serial, role, network config.

## Handoff Log

- 2026-04-16 | Claude (Opus 4.6) | Ticket created. Replaces the earlier Pi-based control-plane proposal; refurb tiny x86 chosen for same price, no ARM portability risk, upgradable. | Next: wait for TKT-0136. When unblocked, stand up Uptime Kuma as first service so probes exist during all subsequent cutover work. | Pitfall: scope creep — resist hosting anything load-bearing on this node; keep it boring and always-alive.

active.title: Design and build Twenty Backend Event Listener for French Registry/SIREN matching
active.created: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context

To preserve the automated intelligence in the transition away from Attio, Twenty must resolve company identities against the French registry (SIREN/SIRET resolution). Instead of building heavy polling jobs, our codebase audit reveals that Twenty's NestJS backend fires a high-performance database batch event model via `@nestjs/event-emitter`. This ticket structures the creation of a native backend module or event listener that captures `"company.created"` events, resolves them against our registry matching pipeline, and enriches the database record asynchronously and natively.

## Current State

*   Twenty utilizes a global `WorkspaceEventEmitter` (at `packages/twenty-server/src/engine/workspace-event-emitter/workspace-event-emitter.ts`) which emits standard database batch events such as `"company.created"` when a company record is inserted via API or manual UX inputs.
*   The registry matching code resides inside `project-ninpo`. We need to design a webhook/API boundary or sidecar interaction that allows Twenty to submit names/domains to our matching pipeline and receive the resolved SIREN metadata.
*   This ticket is blocked by `TKT-0008` (getting the Twenty sandbox fully active on port 3002).

## Task

1.  **Architecture Design:**
    *   Design the matching hook: Determine whether to implement this as a custom NestJS module directly compiled into Twenty's `packages/twenty-server/src/modules/` or as an external webhook receiver in `project-ninpo` subscribed to Twenty's outbound webhook triggers.
    *   *Recommended approach:* A lightweight NestJS listener in Twenty that invokes `project-ninpo`'s matching endpoint via HTTP client (`HttpService` in NestJS) is the cleanest, lowest-maintenance pattern.
2.  **Define Hook Payload & Registry Lookup:**
    *   Register a listener for `company.created` and `company.updated` (when domain/name changes).
    *   Extract the company record `name`, `primary_domain`, or `domain` fields.
    *   Forward these to the `project-ninpo` matching service.
3.  **Implement Enriched Writeback:**
    *   When `project-ninpo` returns the resolved SIREN, tax ID, and address metadata, update the record in Twenty via raw repository query or Twenty's Core API (GraphQL/REST) with the newly mapped details.
4.  **Error Handling & Fallback:**
    *   Handle network timeouts, registry rate limits, and un-matchable international companies gracefully without locking the database thread or throwing fatal errors.

## Execution Notes

*   Do not modify Twenty's core schema migrations directly. Custom fields (like `siren` or `siret`) should be declared via Twenty's Metadata API first, generating dynamic TypeORM schemas automatically.
*   This module should reside under `packages/twenty-server/src/modules/zo-matching` in the local twenty clone, and register in `modules.module.ts`.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Detailed architectural design diagram/plan written mapping the matching hook sequence
- [ ] Custom `zo-matching` NestJS module scaffolded in Twenty's directory
- [ ] Event listener successfully captures the `"company.created"` event payload
- [ ] Listener successfully submits search criteria to `project-ninpo` matching API

## Context

ZO Platform contains highly specialized deal intelligence features, including the Deal RAG transcript analysis, speaker analytics, and automated sourcing signals. active.title: Design and build Twenty Backend Event Listener for Multi-Source Ingestion & Matching
active.created: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context

To preserve the automated company intelligence in the transition away from Attio, Twenty must resolve company records against multiple data sources (e.g. French Registry/SIREN, OpenCorporates, GLEIF, fundraise intel watchlists, and local caches). Instead of coupling Twenty's backend directly to individual data providers, we must abstract this into a **unified Multi-Source Ingestion & Matching pipeline**. 

This ticket designs and builds a high-performance NestJS event listener inside Twenty. It captures `"company.created"` and `"company.updated"` database batch events via `@nestjs/event-emitter` and delegates the match/enrichment request to a central orchestrator endpoint inside `project-ninpo`. The orchestrator executes parallel queries across all active sources, merges the payloads using ZO data normalization rules, and writes a single, canonical enriched record state back to Twenty.

## Current State

*   Twenty utilizes a global `WorkspaceEventEmitter` (at `packages/twenty-server/src/engine/workspace-event-emitter/workspace-event-emitter.ts`) which emits standard database batch events such as `"company.created"` when a company record is inserted via API or manual UX inputs.
*   The former `project-ninpo` database and API contain multi-source matching and cache-lookup systems.
*   We need to establish a lightweight communication protocol (HTTP POST with event payload) where Twenty notifies `project-ninpo` of new or updated companies, triggering the multi-source enrichment sequence asynchronously.
*   This ticket is blocked by `TKT-0008` (getting the Twenty sandbox active on port 3002).

## Task

1.  **Orchestrator Delegation Architecture:**
    *   Design a decoupled, asynchronous NestJS listener in Twenty (`packages/twenty-server/src/modules/zo-ingestion`) that catches `"company.created"` and `"company.updated"` events.
    *   Configure this listener to POST a lightweight event packet (`{ companyId, name, domain, hqCountry }`) to `project-ninpo`'s unified ingestion/matching endpoint.
2.  **`project-ninpo` Multi-Source Coordination:**
    *   Design/extend `project-ninpo`'s orchestrator controller to trigger parallel queries upon receiving the hook:
        - *Source A:* French registry (SIREN/SIRET resolution) if country is FR.
        - *Source B:* OpenCorporates / global entity registry.
        - *Source C:* GLEIF / open database lookup.
        - *Source D:* Sourcing watchlists/intel local databases.
    *   Apply deduplication and property merging rules to compile a clean, unified record profile.
3.  **Unified Writeback Adapter:**
    *   Upon successful resolution and compilation, the `project-ninpo` orchestrator writes the merged record properties (SIREN, open_corp_id, registry_address, employee_count, etc.) back to Twenty via its standard REST/GraphQL metadata APIs.
4.  **Transaction Safety & Infinite Loop Prevention:**
    *   Ensure that Twenty's event listener ignores events where the updater is `project-ninpo` to prevent infinite trigger loops (`Twenty -> project-ninpo -> Twenty -> ...`).
    *   Gracefully handle rate-limits, missing fields, or failed API calls on specific sources without failing the entire ingestion transaction.

## Execution Notes

*   Do not modify Twenty's core schema migrations directly. Dynamic custom fields (like `siren`, `openCorporatesId`, `gleifId`) should be registered first via Twenty's Metadata API.
*   The event listener module in Twenty should reside under `packages/twenty-server/src/modules/zo-ingestion` in the local twenty repository and register in `modules.module.ts`.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Architectural diagram and dataflow mapping for the multi-source orchestrator delegation written
- [ ] Custom `zo-ingestion` NestJS listener module scaffolded inside Twenty's directory
- [ ] Infinite loop prevention mechanism verified (listener successfully ignores writebacks initiated by `project-ninpo`)
- [ ] Event listener successfully submits payload to the `project-ninpo` multi-source ingestion controller
- [ ] `project-ninpo` orchestrator successfully queries FR registry, OpenCorporates, and GLEIF, merging results into a single clean record
- [ ] Parity verified: unified matched data successfully posted back to Twenty sandbox, enriching 10 test company records in real-time
- [ ] Robust error handling logs written for cases where specific datasources are offline or unresolvable

## Handoff Log

*   2026-05-26 | claude-code | Ticket updated and expanded. Transformed from a narrow SIREN-only listener to an abstracted Multi-Source Ingestion & Matching delegator, following feedback to align with project-ninpo's multi-source intelligence requirements.

active.title: Extend twenty-connector with cursor-based pagination and dynamic list tag synchronization
active.created: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context

Our codebase-verified audit of the cloned `twenty` repository shows that Twenty's dynamic API uses cursor-based pagination and record batch limits of 60 records per write request. Additionally, to support list-scoped filtering (which is load-bearing due to MoveCapital's reliance on Attio's 18 named deal flow lists), the `twenty-connector` in `project-ninpo` must support tag-based or relationship-based filters mapped to Twenty's relational schemas. This ticket implements these high-performance pagination and tag filtering layers.

## Current State

*   The `attio-connector` in `project-ninpo` uses Attio's offset-based pagination (max 500 records per page).
*   Twenty's core schema represents lists and groups as dynamic objects or tag metadata relationships.
*   This ticket is blocked by `TKT-0044` (`twenty-connector` scaffold). It implements the actual high-scale synchronization engine of the connector.

## Task

1.  **Cursor-Based Pagination Adapter:**
    *   Build a dynamic generator inside `project-ninpo`’s `twenty-connector/adapter.ts` that parses Twenty's cursor structure (using standard Graphql/REST page info cursors).
    *   Implement high-throughput batching to request records, stepping page-by-page until `hasNextPage` evaluates to false.
2.  **Batch Write Optimization:**
    *   Implement writing chunks: split large records datasets (e.g. 110,946 company updates) into clean 60-record batch writes (matching Twenty's REST/GraphQL batch limits) to bypass payload limits and connection timeouts.
3.  **Named List Tag Synchronization:**
    *   Map Attio's 18 named lists to Twenty's standard database tag relations.
    *   Implement list filtering support: allow the CLI import jobs to parse a `listId` or list tag filter parameter and retrieve *only* records matching that list tag in Twenty (fulfilling the OQ-02 list-scoped handoff prerequisite).

## Execution Notes

*   Keep the sync engine robust: log any skipped, bad-format, or un-parsable records, maintaining high data quality parity.
*   Optimize database reads from Supabase to prevent overlapping connections during batch inserts.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Generator successfully implements cursor-based pagination loop
- [ ] Large dataset writes batched in 60-record chunks with zero transaction failures
- [ ] Mappings implemented between Attio list IDs and Twenty tags/relations
- [ ] CLI import job supports `--list` tag filtering successfully
- [ ] End-to-end integration test: imports 1,000 tagged companies from Twenty sandbox in less than 2 minutes

## Handoff Log

*   2026-05-26 | claude-code | Ticket created. Derived from the codebase audit showing cursor-based API query requirements and batch limit structures.

active.title: Deploy Vexa in Gordon-Stack Compose Sandbox
active.created: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context
As part of MoveCapital's platform transition, we are self-hosting all services on T418 under `gordon-stack` to achieve full data sovereignty. Vexa is selected as the open-source self-hosted meeting bot and transcription engine to replace manual or third-party recording capture. This ticket covers the containerized deployment of Vexa inside the local `gordon-stack` Docker Compose sandbox on T418. Without this sandbox deployment, we cannot validate the meeting bot or its transcription pipelines locally.

## Current State
`gordon-stack` contains active Postgres and Redis instances. Vexa has been successfully cloned locally into `01_Repos/vexa`. Vexa's architecture consists of five core microservices: `api-gateway`, `meeting-api` (Python FastAPI), `admin-api`, `runtime-api` (container orchestrator), and a Next.js `dashboard`. It also backing services `whisper` (transcription-service) and an S3-compatible backend (MinIO) or local filesystem for finalized recording storage.

## Task
Configure and deploy a self-hosted Vexa instance in the `gordon-stack` Docker Compose sandbox on T418:
1. **Scaffold Services:** Integrate Vexa's five microservices (`api-gateway`, `meeting-api`, `admin-api`, `runtime-api`, `dashboard`) into the `gordon-stack` docker-compose template or compile a standalone `docker-compose.vexa.yml` that mounts to `gordon-stack`'s shared networks.
2. **Backing Resources Connection:** Connect Vexa services to the shared PostgreSQL instance (creating a dedicated `vexa` database schema) and the shared Redis instance.
3. **Environment Setup:** Configure the `.env` using MinIO or local storage as the `STORAGE_BACKEND` for WebM recordings. Expose the dashboard on port `3000` and the api-gateway on port `8056`.
4. **Vexa Lite / CPU Evaluation:** Configure the stack to run in Vexa Lite mode (single container / CPU-only transcription) or standalone CPU mode to avoid immediate GPU dependencies during evaluation on T418.

## Execution Notes
* **Network Integration:** Ensure Vexa containers reside on the same Docker networks as existing gordon-stack app services (e.g., `gordon-network`) so they can resolve local DNS.
* **Storage Pathing:** Ensure `LOCAL_STORAGE_DIR` is set within `02_Runtime/vexa/storage` rather than `/tmp` to persist WebM recordings across stack restarts.
* **Secure Defaults:** Use strong passwords and isolated schemas for the PostgreSQL connection.
* **DO NOT** commit live S3 keys or DB credentials into the repository; use the sandbox `.env` and `generate-envs.sh` script patterns.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Vexa services compile and spin up successfully via `docker compose up -d` in the sandbox.
- [ ] Vexa dashboard is accessible in the browser on `http://localhost:3000`.
- [ ] Vexa API gateway responds to `GET /health` or `GET /meetings` on `http://localhost:8056`.
- [ ] A test database connection is established and verified in `meeting-api` logs.

## Handoff Log
- 2026-05-26 | Antigravity | Created ticket and detailed Vexa compose sandbox deployment task | Sandbox ticket scaffolded. | Ready for sandbox configuration scripting. | Ensure port conflicts with existing services are audited.

active.title: Build Vexa-to-RAG Ingestion Pipeline Script
active.created: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context
MoveCapital maintains a RAG pipeline (`transcript-rag-api`) that embeds call transcripts into vectors to support semantic search and Q&A inside `movecapital-platform`. Currently, transcripts are uploaded manually or via complex n8n steps. Vexa captures calls and transcribes them into highly structured speaker-attributed segments. This ticket covers building an ingestion pipeline script inside `transcript-rag-api` to automatically ingest and embed Vexa transcripts when calls end.

## Current State
`transcript-rag-api` currently utilizes a Qdrant collection and is scheduled to be migrated to a pgvector collection (`TKT-0051`). Vexa v0.10.4 aggregates Redis transcript segments into PostgreSQL and triggers the `"meeting.completed"` webhook event when a meeting concludes. The full transcript (with word-level timestamps and speaker events) is retrievable via `GET /transcripts/{platform}/{native_meeting_id}`.

## Task
Design and implement the Vexa-to-RAG ingestion adapter inside `transcript-rag-api`:
1. **Webhook Endpoint:** Implement a webhook listener route (e.g. `POST /ingest/vexa`) in `transcript-rag-api` that listens for Vexa's `meeting.completed` payload.
2. **HMAC Signature Verification:** Validate incoming webhook payloads using the shared `webhook_secret` via HMAC-SHA256 signature verification in headers to secure the endpoint.
3. **Fetch Transcript Segments:** Fetch the full speaker-attributed segments for the completed meeting by making a secure REST call to `GET /transcripts/{platform}/{native_meeting_id}` using the internal meeting API credentials.
4. **Context-Aware Chunking:** Implement a chunking strategy that splits the transcript:
   - Chunk size: by speaker turns or time windows (e.g., 2–3 minute windows).
   - Inject context: Prefix each chunk with the speaker name (e.g., `"[Speaker: Alice]: let's discuss Q1 results"`), meeting name, and date to preserve diarization context for vector retrieval.
5. **pgvector Embedding & Persistence:** Generate vector embeddings for the chunks using the local Ollama instance or the configured cloud model, and persist them inside pgvector (storing chunk text, meeting ID, platform, speaker, and timestamps).

## Execution Notes
* **Idempotency:** Guard against double ingestion. Check if the meeting ID already exists in the database before processing the webhook.
* **Timestamp Support:** Ensure absolute timestamps are calculated and stored in pgvector using the meeting's `start_time` and segment relative offset times.
* **Fallback Mechanisms:** If the webhook delivery fails or transcript-rag-api is offline during webhook delivery, ensure the script supports an on-demand manual trigger via a CLI command passing the meeting ID.
* **DO NOT** process transcripts that are marked `redacted` in Vexa.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Ingestion endpoint `POST /ingest/vexa` is created and verified.
- [ ] Webhook HMAC signature verification is implemented and tested.
- [ ] Script successfully fetches structured segments and speaker metadata from Vexa.
- [ ] Transcripts are chunked, speaker contexts are prefixed, and vectors are successfully persisted in pgvector.

## Handoff Log
- 2026-05-26 | Antigravity | Scaffolding the Vexa-to-RAG ingestion pipeline ticket | Webhook listener and chunking adapter planned. | Ready for adapter scripting. | Ensure chunking keeps speaker identifiers attached.

active.title: Implement Twenty CRM Participant Matching Hook
active.created: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context
Vexa logs call participants as they appear in the meeting (extracting names and emails from DOM elements), but has no built-in CRM integration. To align transcripts with portfolio company records, we need a post-processing layer that maps participant details back to Companies and Persons inside Twenty CRM. This ticket covers implementing a matching handler inside `project-ninpo` to map participant emails to Twenty CRM entities when calls end.

## Current State
`project-ninpo` contains a multi-source company matching orchestrator. Twenty CRM is deployed inside a compose sandbox (`TKT-0008`). Vexa v0.10.4 aggregates participant emails in the meeting database and supports a webhook Completion event containing the array of participant names and emails under `meeting.data.participants` and `meeting.data.speaker_events`. It also supports `POST_MEETING_HOOKS` (list of internal callback URLs triggered after finalization).

## Task
Implement the CRM domain-matching event handler in `project-ninpo`:
1. **Hook Registration:** Configure `project-ninpo`'s callback URL inside Vexa's `POST_MEETING_HOOKS` or set it as a webhook recipient for `meeting.completed` events.
2. **Scrub Participant Emails:** Extract the list of participant emails from the incoming completed event payload (filtering out the bot's own email and junk domain entries like anonymous/Google Meet device fallbacks).
3. **Twenty CRM Lookup:** For each valid email, extract the domain (e.g. `company.com`) and run the matching search inside Twenty CRM:
   - **Company Search:** Query Twenty's Company model to search for a match against the domain.
   - **Person Search:** Query Twenty's Person model to locate existing records matching the participant's full email.
4. **CRM Association:**
   - If a Company record matches, link the call transcript and meeting ID to the Twenty Company record.
   - If a Person record matches, link the participant as an attendee of the meeting inside Twenty CRM.
   - If a Person record is missing but belongs to a matched Company domain, auto-scaffold a draft Person record in Twenty under that Company account.

## Execution Notes
* **Junk Domain Scrubber:** Explicitly ignore public mail providers (e.g., `gmail.com`, `outlook.com`, `yahoo.com`, `hotmail.com`) during domain-matching company lookups to avoid linking meetings to generic accounts.
* **Batch Lookup Efficiency:** Implement parallelized queries or batched API calls to Twenty to avoid timeout delays when processing calls with large attendee lists.
* **Logging & Telemetry:** Log all successful lookups, mapping results, and unmatched participants so operators can manually review anomalous records.
* **Error Resilience:** Ensure Twenty CRM API failures do not crash the handler; enqeue a retry or write the unlinked transcript to a dead-letter log.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Participant matcher hook is successfully registered and receives completed call payloads.
- [ ] The matching engine ignores junk/public mail domains and filters bot identifiers.
- [ ] Participant domains are resolved against Twenty Companies, and individuals are mapped to Twenty Persons.
- [ ] Call transcripts and attendee connections are written back and verified in Twenty's database.

## Handoff Log
- 2026-05-26 | Antigravity | Scaffolding the Twenty CRM participant domain-matching ticket | Domain lookup flow and Twenty API write-back planned. | Ready for matching handler script implementation. | Verify public domain filter logic.

active.title: Integrate Infisical Universal Auth (Machine Identities) into project-ninpo and movecapital-platform startup wrappers
active.created: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context
Infisical has deprecated static `SERVICE_TOKEN` and API key authentication in favor of Machine Identities (Universal Auth). In order to securely manage and inject configuration parameters into our local microservices (`transcript-rag-api`, `deal-rag`, etc.), we must replace hardcoded, machine-local static `.env` files with a dynamic environment injection wrapper. This wrapper will fetch and inject secrets on-demand at application boot, hardening our runtime services against static secret exposure.

## Current State
Services inside `project-ninpo` and `movecapital-platform` currently load secrets from uncommitted, static local `.env` files on disk. The migration to a three-node x86 architecture is planned (TKT-0135), and Infisical vault deployment on Node-2 is tracked under TKT-0092. Once Infisical is live on Node-2, we need a standard, production-grade wrapper to dynamically stream these secrets to our runtimes.

## Task
1. Create a machine identity in the Infisical vault (e.g. `zo-platform-identity`) representing our platform runtimes.
2. Configure Universal Auth for this identity, obtaining a Client ID and Client Secret. Configure these in the host environment or as host-level Docker Secrets.
3. Grant the machine identity read permissions to the appropriate environment folders and paths inside Infisical using access control policies.
4. Scaffold a startup wrapper script (`infisical-bootstrap.sh` or equivalent wrapper) in `project-ninpo` and `movecapital-platform` that:
   - Evaluates whether Infisical is reachable and authenticates via Universal Auth.
   - Fetches and injects decrypted secrets dynamically at boot using the `infisical run` command, or renders secrets locally using an Infisical Agent daemon sidecar.
   - Falls back gracefully to standard, local mock `.env` file reading if running in offline development/sandbox mode.
5. Update compose files and application startup scripts to trigger via this new wrapper, removing raw `.env` dependencies from production and staging runtime environments.

## Execution Notes
- **Do not commit live Client Secrets or Client IDs** to the repository. Maintain them inside host-local config folders or Docker Secrets.
- Utilize the **Infisical Agent** sidecar with file sinks (`agent-config.yaml`) in higher-volume microservice contexts to avoid thundering-herd API request bursts on Infisical's Postgres DB during batch container restarts.
- Ensure the wrapper outputs helpful, redacted log notifications during authentication failures or connectivity timeouts.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Infisical Machine Identity successfully created in the vault with least-privilege scoping policies.
- [ ] Universal Auth startup wrapper script written and integrated into `project-ninpo` and `movecapital-platform`.
- [ ] Dynamic process injection verified (application correctly receives decrypted environment parameters).
- [ ] Microservices boot successfully without static `.env` files on disk.
- [ ] Infisical Agent sidecar caching configured and verified for performance scale.

## Handoff Log
- 2026-05-26 | Antigravity | Created active ticket detailing Universal Auth integration roadmap based on Infisical codebase audit findings. | Ticket spawned in ACTIVE folder. | Awaiting Infisical live status (TKT-0092) to execute implementation. | None.

active.title: Harden docker-backup script to securely backup Infisical PostgreSQL volume and recovery keys
active.created: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context
Infisical uses multi-layer symmetric envelope encryption where all database secrets, organization data keys, and project data keys are stored as ciphertext in PostgreSQL. These are encrypted with keys derived from `ENCRYPTION_KEY` and `ROOT_ENCRYPTION_KEY` environment variables. If a backup is taken of the Postgres volume or logical SQL dump, but the host environment variables are lost, the backup is completely unrecoverable and bricked. Conversely, writing these key environment variables in plain text adjacent to the database backup compromises the security boundary of the vault.

## Current State
The current backup scripts (`docker_backup.ps1` in `gordon-stack` and `backup.ps1` in `docker-backup`) capture the live `.env` files in plain text if the `-IncludeSensitiveConfig` switch is enabled. This places raw keys and DB dumps together in plain text under `C:\docker-backups\<date>`.

## Task
1. Harden the PowerShell backup scripts (`docker_backup.ps1` in `gordon-stack` and `backup.ps1` in `docker-backup`) to perform pre-flight checks on Infisical key configurations:
   - Identify if Infisical variables (`ENCRYPTION_KEY`, `ROOT_ENCRYPTION_KEY`, `AUTH_SECRET`) are present.
   - Validate key properties (e.g., verify that `ENCRYPTION_KEY` is a non-empty string, and if Base64 encoded, validates against expected block lengths).
2. Refactor the script to secure the `.env` backup:
   - Do not write the `.env` file containing Infisical keys in plain text to the backup directory.
   - Implement an asymmetric encryption routine in PowerShell (using native `.NET` `System.Security.Cryptography` or an `openssl` command) that encrypts the sensitive `.env` file using a configured offline public key (e.g. RSA or ECC public key certificate).
   - Save only the encrypted payload (e.g., `.env.enc` or `.env.pgp`) to the host backup root.
3. Ensure that the Postgres container's logical SQL dump is successfully taken, and that both the volume tarball and the logical SQL dump are verified for size and integrity.
4. Document the exact restoration runbook (decryption using the private key + database import) inside a `RECOVERY_DRILL.md` artifact in both repositories.

## Execution Notes
- Pre-flight checks should gracefully report warnings but fail-stop the backup job if a critical key is defined in compose but unreadable.
- Store the public key certificate used for encryption securely in `02_Runtime/` or passed via parameter, keeping the private key completely offline.
- Avoid using inline plaintext secrets inside the backup log files.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Pre-flight checks successfully validate the presence of Infisical root keys.
- [ ] Sensitive `.env` backup is saved in encrypted format (`.env.enc`) and verified to contain no plain text keys.
- [ ] Asymmetric encryption executes natively or via stable toolchain call.
- [ ] The Postgres database logical SQL dump and volume tarballs are created and verified.
- [ ] Restoration runbook written, verified, and tested with a manual decryption drill.

## Handoff Log
- 2026-05-26 | Antigravity | Spawned active ticket detailing the hardening of docker-backup scripts to securely capture and encrypt Infisical keys. | Ticket written to ACTIVE folder. | Ready for implementation. | None.

active.title: Configure Restic/pgBackRest Backups & Scaffold Restore Drill Agent
active.created: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context

A robust backup strategy is the ultimate line of defense against catastrophic data loss, database corruption, or ransomware. For the ZO Platform, backups must be highly secure, client-side encrypted, space-efficient (deduplicated), and automatically replicated. We require client-side encrypted backups using **Restic** (or **Kopia**) backed up to both local NAS and offsite S3-compatible storage.

However, untested backups are not backups at all. Too many platforms discover their backups are corrupted only during a real disaster recovery. This ticket configures our production backups and designs the AI **"Restore Drill" Agent**—a scheduled system that automatically pulls the latest backup weekly, decrypts it inside a throwaway database container, runs `VACUUM ANALYZE` integrity checks, and logs verified restore audits to guarantee recovery readiness.

## Current State

*   Existing database backup scripts are manual, locally stored on the host, and lack encryption or replication to NAS/S3.
*   Host directory ownership and non-root execution permissions need to be handled during backup execution (Node-2).
*   No automated restore drill or integrity checking is configured.
*   This ticket is a high-priority baseline security requirement.

## Task

1.  **Configure Restic/Kopia Backup Stack:**
    *   Initialize a secure **Restic** (or **Kopia**) backup repository.
    *   Implement client-side AES-256 encryption. Keep keys secure and completely outside the repositories.
    *   Draft the backup orchestrator script (`ops/scripts/backup-platform.sh` or `.py`) targeting:
        *   PostgreSQL databases (Supabase / local engines).
        *   Keycloak configurations.
        *   Vector database storage (pgvector).
2.  **Automate Offsite & NAS Targets:**
    *   Set up backup destinations targeting a local NAS mount and a secondary, offsite S3-compatible bucket (e.g. Backblaze B2, AWS S3, Cloudflare R2).
    *   Automate pruning and snapshot rotation using systemd timers or crontab on Node-2. Use standard retention rules (e.g. `--keep-daily 7 --keep-weekly 4 --keep-monthly 12`).
3.  **Design "Restore Drill" Agent:**
    *   Create a weekly automated verification workflow (`ops/scripts/restore-drill-agent.sh` or `.py`).
    *   The script must spin up a completely network-isolated PostgreSQL Docker container (`--network none`) on a separate control tier node (Node-3).
4.  **Execute Automated Restore & Integrity Auditing:**
    *   The drill agent pulls the latest snapshot from the backup repository, decrypts it using keys fetched from the Infisical vault, and restores the database schema and tables.
    *   Run integrity checks inside the restored database:
        *   Execute `VACUUM ANALYZE` to check for index/page corruption.
        *   Execute row-count checks and structural integrity validations on primary tables (`company_identity`, `deal_identity`, `person_identity`).
    *   Generate a detailed, timestamped markdown audit report (`verification/backups/drills/restore-audit-YYYY-MM-DD.md`).
5.  **Failure Alerts & Logging:**
    *   If the restore fails, the key is missing, or database corruption is detected during the vacuum, halt execution, preserve log outputs, and dispatch an urgent high-severity alert via our Telegram/Slack webhook.

## Execution Notes

*   Never run the restore drill on the production database host or inside a production-facing network segment.
*   Keep backup repository passwords and S3 access tokens securely in the Infisical vault or highly protected, non-committed configuration files.
*   Do not leave temporary throwaway PG containers running; ensure the script executes cleanups in a `finally` block or shell `trap`.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Restic/Kopia backup script initialized, configured with client-side encryption, and tested successfully
- [ ] Automated backup rotation and S3/NAS replication active (weekly crons/timers confirmed)
- [ ] AI "Restore Drill" Agent script scaffolded and verified on a test database snapshot
- [ ] Drill execution successfully restores data to a network-isolated throwaway Docker container
- [ ] Database integrity checking (`VACUUM ANALYZE` and row counts) fully verified in the restore script
- [ ] Weekly timestamped restore audit reports successfully written to the verification directory
- [ ] Error notification and warning alert logic verified via simulated restore failure

## Handoff Log

*   2026-05-26 | antigravity-ide | Ticket created. Configures AES-256 encrypted backups (NAS + S3) and designs a weekly automated restore drill agent to guarantee data recoverability.

active.title: Custom Open WebUI Backend Plugin calling ZO Middleware
active.created: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context

Open WebUI has been adopted as ZO's primary AI assistant chat interface in Phase 6 (Intelligence). To provide a unified platform experience, users must be able to trigger our backend intent routing and specialized agent swarms directly from the chat. Our codebase audit confirms that Open WebUI supports a dynamic Python-based Tool plugin architecture. This ticket handles creating a native backend plugin that integrates with the ZO Middleware API, forwarding user prompts along with auth session variables and streaming live swarm states to the chat window.

## Current State

*   Open WebUI is cloned in the workspace at `C:\Users\LENOVO\Workspace\01_Repos\open-webui`.
*   Plugins and custom tools are dynamically loaded at runtime using `backend/open_webui/utils/plugin.py`.
*   The Tool execution context supports injecting FastAPI `Request`, `__user__` (holding email and metadata), and `__event_emitter__` (for live WebSocket status updates).
*   No integration exists between Open WebUI and the local ZO Middleware REST service (to run at `http://zo-middleware:8000`).

## Task

1.  **Develop Python Tool Module:**
    *   Scaffold a native Python Tool (`open_webui/tools/zo_swarm_router.py`) defining a `Tools` class.
    *   Expose a method `query_zo_swarm` with complete type hints and a detailed docstring so Open WebUI's spec compiler (`get_tool_specs()`) exposes it correctly to the LLM.
    *   Parameterize the method with `prompt`, `__user__`, and `__event_emitter__`.
2.  **Middleware Communication:**
    *   Implement an asynchronous POST request using `aiohttp` to `http://zo-middleware:8000/api/v1/swarm/route`.
    *   Extract the active user's identity details from `__user__.get("email")` and forward it inside the payload as a security/tracking context.
    *   Configure connection timeouts up to 120 seconds to allow complex agent swarm chains to complete.
3.  **Real-Time Status Indicators:**
    *   As the request completes, trigger progress updates to the frontend using the `__event_emitter__` callable:
        ```python
        await __event_emitter__({
            "type": "status",
            "data": {"description": "Routing intent to ZO Agent Swarm...", "done": False}
        })
        ```
    *   Ensure proper exception handling to clean up the spinner status if the middleware service crashes.

## Execution Notes

*   Open WebUI must be running as a sidecar in the same Docker network segment as the `zo-middleware` container.
*   Do not hardcode API endpoints; utilize a Valve configuration class to support environment overrides (`http://zo-middleware:8000` by default).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Native Python tool registered successfully in Open WebUI admin panel
- [ ] Swarm routing tool accurately exposed to LLM context via dynamic specs
- [ ] Active user's email forwarded to the ZO Middleware container inside authorization payload
- [ ] Real-time event emitter status indicators visually verify swarm states in chat UI
- [ ] Tool handles long-running swarm queries (up to 120s) with proper network timeout recoveries

## Handoff Log

- 2026-05-26 | antigravity | Ticket created. Triggered by Open WebUI codebase audit mapping.

active.title: Configure movecapital-platform Right-Drawer Embedding of Open WebUI
active.created: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context

To make the user experience premium, the AI assistant sidecar (Open WebUI) must be instantly accessible without forcing the user to switch tabs or leave their current deal/company context. This ticket handles embedding Open WebUI directly inside a slide-out drawer panel (Right Drawer UI widget) in the `movecapital-platform` dashboard shell. It requires optimizing Open WebUI's UI layouts for tight sidebar framing and configuring shared cookie scopes to maintain seamless authentication.

## Current State

*   The platform shell design includes scaffolding for collapsible sidebar drawers (under blocked design ticket `TKT-0067`).
*   Open WebUI has full Svelte page routes but renders left sidebars, history panels, and main headers by default, which takes too much horizontal space for a narrow drawer frame.
*   Authentication cookies (`token`, `oui-session`) are scoped locally, which prevents single sign-on framing unless configured correctly.

## Task

1.  **Configure Compact Framing View:**
    *   Optimize Svelte layouts inside `open-webui` (or establish a clean embed-only route like `/embed/chat` or append URL params e.g. `?embed=true`) that hides the left history sidebar, top header navigation, and default chat margins.
    *   Styling must utilize Harmonious HSL tailormade colors and smooth micro-animations to align cleanly with our platform's dark/glassmorphism design theme.
2.  **Iframe Integration & Layout:**
    *   Mount an `<iframe />` inside the Right-Drawer React widget in `movecapital-platform`.
    *   Map standard postMessage communication bridges between the parent platform and the framed assistant to enable context injection (e.g. passing the currently open company ID, deal ID, or transcripts directly to the assistant's context).
3.  **Same-Session Cookie Binding:**
    *   Configure `WEBUI_AUTH_COOKIE_SAME_SITE` and `WEBUI_AUTH_COOKIE_SECURE` in Open WebUI's `env.py` to allow cookie transmission in nested contexts.
    *   Define parent cookie sharing domains (`.zo.internal`) so the framed Open WebUI inherits the Keycloak session automatically from the parent shell.
4.  **Framing Security Audits:**
    *   Audit and restrict Content Security Policies (CSP) and `X-Frame-Options` inside Open WebUI (`backend/open_webui/main.py`) to restrict framing exclusively to authorized parent platform origins.

## Execution Notes

*   Verify scrollbar mechanics to eliminate double scrollbar layouts or UX clipping within the narrow sliding drawer view.
*   Bridge user contexts via query parameters or postMessage hooks to allow context-aware assistant interactions.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Optimized layout route (e.g., `/embed/chat`) created in Open WebUI Svelte layer with hidden navigation frames
- [ ] React slide-out Right-Drawer UI successfully mounts the framed Open WebUI widget
- [ ] Session cookie sharing bindings authenticated cleanly inside the frame without manual re-login
- [ ] Iframe X-Frame-Options and CORS headers configured securely to block unauthorized domains
- [ ] Parent-to-Frame context communication verified (e.g., opening a deal details page successfully seeds deal metadata into the assistant drawer)

## Handoff Log

- 2026-05-26 | antigravity | Ticket created. Triggered by Open WebUI codebase audit mapping.

active.title: DB Schema Extensions for Sourcing Signals, Relational Networks, and Talent Metrics
active.created: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context

A visual audit of `Move UX.pdf` against the active codebase reveals that the high-fidelity sourcing, pipeline, and contact networks render critical data attributes currently missing in our Drizzle database schemas. To prepare the platform for real backend integrations (such as the Twenty CRM events listener, Vexa transcript sweeps, and comps pipeline scoring), the database schema must be extended. This ticket handles creating a dedicated sourcing schema and extending existing identity, people, and scoring tables with dense analytical attributes.

## Current State

*   Sourcing data in `app/app/sourcing/page.tsx` relies entirely on frontend static fallback mappings. There are no tables for raw/triage signals, confidence scores, or outreach draft helpers.
*   The `company_scores` and `scoring_inputs` tables in `src/db/schema/scoring.ts` store IPC spread and financial figures but miss specialized talent metrics (talent density top-percentiles, poached engineer counts, team scaling velocity).
*   The contacts and roles tables in `src/db/schema/people.ts` and `src/db/schema/deals.ts` do not track network prioritizations, outreach history, or warm/cold decay triggers.

## Task

1.  **Create [sourcing.ts](file:///C:/Users/LENOVO/Workspace/01_Repos/zo/services/ninpo-api/src/db/schema/sourcing.ts) Schema:**
    *   Define `sourcingSignals` table:
        *   `id`: UUID primary key.
        *   `companyName`: Text (not null).
        *   `announcedOn`: Date or timestamp.
        *   `amount`: BigInt (number mode).
        *   `currency`: Text (default 'EUR').
        *   `roundType`: Text (e.g. 'Series B').
        *   `confidence`: Integer (0 to 100).
        *   `hqCity` & `hqCountry`: Text.
        *   `sector` & `subSector`: Text.
        *   `status`: Text (default 'triage').
        *   `linkedinDraft` & `emailDraft`: Text (for copy-ready outreach templates).
    *   Define `sourcingSynthesis` table linked to `sourcingSignals`:
        *   `id`: UUID primary key.
        *   `signalId`: UUID reference.
        *   `moatTitle` & `moatText`: Text (e.g. "Proprietary Edge Architecture (Critical Moat)").
        *   `validationTitle` & `validationText`: Text (e.g. "Tier 1 Investor Validation").
        *   `talentTitle` & `talentText`: Text (e.g. "Adjacent Talent Magnet").
        *   `marketSentimentIndex`: Integer (0 to 100).
        *   `provenanceSources`: JSONB (array of strings, e.g. Crunchbase, TechCrunch).
        *   `peerMarketCompanies`: JSONB (array of objects with company name and stage tags).
2.  **Modify [people.ts](file:///C:/Users/LENOVO/Workspace/01_Repos/zo/services/ninpo-api/src/db/schema/people.ts):**
    *   Add columns to support network velocity dashboards:
        *   `warmNodeFlag`: Boolean (default false).
        *   `coldThreadFlag`: Boolean (default false).
        *   `decayRatePercent`: Real.
        *   `lastInteractionAgeDays`: Integer.
        *   `outreachSuccessRate`: Real (0.0 to 100.0).
        *   `intelligenceTriggerText`: Text (e.g. "Closed $45M Series B for Neon Systems. Your last interaction was 8 months ago...").
3.  **Modify [scoring.ts](file:///C:/Users/LENOVO/Workspace/01_Repos/zo/services/ninpo-api/src/db/schema/scoring.ts):**
    *   Extend `scoringInputs` with corporate intelligence and talent fields:
        *   `talentDensityPercentile`: Integer (e.g. top 1% indicator).
        *   `teamGrowthRate`: Real (e.g. team scaling 12% faster).
        *   `poachedEngineerCount`: Integer (e.g. 12 engineers from Starship Logistics).
4.  **Export & Register Schemas:**
    *   Import and re-export the new sourcing schema inside `services/ninpo-api/src/db/schema/index.ts`.
    *   Generate a Drizzle SQL migration and verify compatibility with existing Postgres setups.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] New `sourcing.ts` schema file created defining signal, synthesis, and peer market tables
- [ ] `people.ts` updated with warm/cold metrics, decay rates, and outreach templates
- [ ] `scoring.ts` extended with first-class talent density and poach metrics
- [ ] New schemas registered in the main `index.ts` schema entrypoint
- [ ] DB migration successfully compiled using `npx drizzle-kit generate`

## Handoff Log

- 2026-05-26 | antigravity | Ticket created. Triggered by DB Schema and high-fidelity UX audit gap analysis.

active.title: High-Fidelity Admin Console & Data Health Operations Workspace
active.created: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)

## Context

The "Admin Console" and "Data Health" interfaces currently consist of bare toggles, form fields, and raw developer socket exceptions. This contradicts Page 10 of `Move UX.pdf`, which represents an elegant "Operator Console / Terminal v4.2". The designed UI displays global API uptime metrics (99.98%), dynamic vector DB / scraper latencies, Bloomberg Terminal VC/SEC sync status lights, failed jobs logs, LLM quota limits, CPU/RAM/Net logs, and SuperAdmin rosters. This ticket handles building out the high-fidelity admin dashboard.

## Current State

*   The Next.js folders `app/app/admin/` and `app/app/data-health/` render `AdminConsoleWorkspace.tsx` and `DataHealthWorkspace.tsx`.
*   These workspaces consist of plain tables listing raw feature keys and override database forms without any system status lights or telemetry dashboarding.
*   Developer logs (e.g. `127.0.0.1:8084`) are printed directly to the frontend.

## Task

1.  **Redesign [AdminConsoleWorkspace.tsx](file:///C:/Users/LENOVO/Workspace/01_Repos/zo/app/components/AdminConsoleWorkspace.tsx):**
    *   Implement the "Operator Console / Terminal v4.2" layout matching Page 10:
        *   **Telemetry Bar:** System Load (`24.2%`), API Uptime (`99.98%`), CPU (`12%`), RAM (`4.2 GB / 16 GB`), Network (`1.2 GBps`).
        *   **Service Status Table:** Rows for Vector DB (Pinecone) [Healthy, 12ms], LLM API (OpenAI) [Degraded, 840ms], Web Scraper [Healthy, 42ms], Audit Logger [Healthy, 4ms].

        *   **Quotas & Limits Controller:** Interactive controls to adjust Daily LLM API Calls (`10,000`), Max Sourcing Depth (`5 levels`), and Cache Retention (`30 days`). Add working "Update Configuration" and "Refresh All" buttons.
        *   **Danger Zone Panel:** Flush Cache & Rebuild Index actions with explicit confirmation modal boxes.

2.  **Redesign [DataHealthWorkspace.tsx](file:///C:/Users/LENOVO/Workspace/01_Repos/zo/app/components/DataHealthWorkspace.tsx):**
    *   Replace raw developer-level logs with a beautiful status translation component that maps network exceptions into user-friendly diagnostic cards.
3.  **Integrate API Probes:**
    *   Wire these views to read live telemetry from `app/lib/runtime-health.ts` (polling local Postgres, Ollama sidecar, and Keycloak scopes).

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `AdminConsoleWorkspace.tsx` successfully redesigned into high-fidelity "Operator Console" UI
- [ ] Telemetry status bars render live CPU, RAM, and network capacity indicators
- [ ] Data Source status lights clearly reflect healthy/degraded/failed connections

active.title: Interactive Meeting Player & French Audio Transcript Player
active.created: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.updated: Tue May 26 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
active.body:
## Context

Page 22 of the design document represents an elite "Operations / Meeting Discussion Pipeline Player". It exhibits a full audio/video discussion workspace featuring timeline controllers, speaker-labeled paragraphs mapped to timing, dynamic transcript streams, participants lists, and a side-panel "Ask ZO" chat assistant contextually bound to the meeting transcript. Currently, `CaptureHubWorkspace.tsx` only lists text files and conversion statuses. This ticket handles building the interactive playback and transcription player.

## Current State

*   The Next.js folder `app/app/capture/` loads a static view `CaptureHubWorkspace.tsx`.
*   Clicking captured audio or recorded meetings fails to open a player; instead, it is a dead end.
*   The system lacks timing-mapped transcript structures and French audio transcript rendering pathways.

## Task

1.  **Create [app/app/capture/[id]/page.tsx](file:///C:/Users/LENOVO/Workspace/01_Repos/zo/app/app/capture/%5Bid%5D/page.tsx) Route:**
    *   Create a dynamic Next.js page that fetches meeting transcripts and metadata from the database (e.g. from Vexa voice note backups or local fixtures).
2.  **Build High-Fidelity Discussion Player (`MeetingPlayerWorkspace`):**
    *   Scaffold a premium player matching Page 22:
        *   **Player Control Panel:** Renders an audio playback strip with timestamp counters (`00:42 / 1:15:39`), forward/rewind jump triggers (`-10s`, `+10s`), play/pause controls, and volume parameters.
        *   **Speakers Transcript Panel:**
            *   Map paragraphs to precise timestamps and speaker tags:
                *   `BF (Benque Frederic) [3:37]`: "allô allô Frédéric bonsoir..." (French speech stream).
                *   `MH (Malaussena Herve) [3:51]`: "on va finir un col avec sophie..."
                *   `TS (Thomas Sancinena) [4:03]`: "Tom, est-ce que tu peux partager le mail pipeline s'il te plaît..."
                *   `ET (Enzo Terrier) [4:26]`: "Autonomous Teaming, Fred, c'est une société qui a été fondée par..."
            *   Add active speaker highlighting that syncs with the audio timeline.
        *   **Right Rail - Session Context & "Ask ZO":**
            *   *Participants List:* Fred, Clement de Coudenhove, Benque Frederic, Malaussena Herve, Thomas Sancinena, Enzo Terrier.
            *   *Meeting metadata:* Date (Oct 24, 2023), Duration (01:15:39).
            *   *AI Summary report:* Structured paragraphs detailing the team's discussion on defense tech pivots in DACH, Finnish fusion startups, and liquidity.
            *   *Ask ZO session assistant:* Input field contextually bound to the transcript. Users can query "Summarize the Finnish fusion discussion" and retrieve exact quotes.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Route `app/app/capture/[id]/page.tsx` created and registers successfully
- [ ] Premium discussion player layout matches Page 22 of the design document
- [ ] Timeline playback bar accurately displays progress, total duration, and handles jump commands
- [ ] Transcription parser renders speaker-labeled sections with correct timestamps and text
- [ ] "Ask ZO" search panel returns precise context-bound citations from the transcript
- [ ] Audio sync maps highlight active paragraphs in real-time during simulation

## Handoff Log

- 2026-05-26 | antigravity | Ticket created. Triggered by audio transcript and meeting player gap.
- 2026-05-26 | codex | During TKT-0159, `app/app/capture/[id]/page.tsx` was added as a live PoC route and smoke-tested successfully. The original route-missing assumption in this ticket is stale; the real player, transcript sync, and Ask ZO behavior remain unimplemented.

active.title: Finalize monorepo transition and restore clean git state

## Context

The ZO repo contains a large dirty working tree caused by an uncommitted monorepo transition that relocated the Ninpo API, web package, and shared contracts out of `data/` into top-level monorepo roots. The file move appears structurally complete, but git still reports tracked deletions, untracked replacement paths, stale ticket index state, and migration documentation that was never landed as a clean commit.

## Current State

- Replacement roots exist locally at `services/ninpo-api`, `app/ninpo-web`, and `packages/contracts`.
- Repo governance files, README, provenance docs, migration verification notes, and active ticket metadata were updated locally but not committed.
- `WIP/tickets/TICKETS.md` still reports `Next ticket number: TKT-0153` even though `TKT-0153` through `TKT-0157` already exist in `ACTIVE/`.

## Task

1. Verify whether the monorepo relocation is structurally complete and identify any remaining references that would block landing it.
2. Update ticket/index metadata needed to govern the cleanup pass and reflect the already-created active tickets.
3. Stage the intentional monorepo transition changes, including moved code, root workspace files, and supporting documentation.
4. Validate the landed state as far as the local environment allows, noting any skipped verification such as missing dependencies.
5. Commit the cleanup so `main` returns to a coherent, reviewable state.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] `WIP/tickets/TICKETS.md` reflects `TKT-0153` through `TKT-0158` and the next free number
- [ ] Intentional move from `data/` to top-level monorepo roots is staged coherently
- [ ] Repo reaches a clean or intentionally-explained git state after commit
- [ ] Verification status and any remaining risks are recorded

## Handoff Log

Initial assessment: structure appears complete enough to land, but index/ticket metadata and repo cleanliness are inconsistent. Next: update ticket index, stage intentional transition files, verify, and commit.
- 2026-05-26 | codex | Verified the relocation is structurally complete: legacy `data/apps/api`, `data/apps/web`, and `data/packages/contracts` now exist at `services/ninpo-api`, `app/ninpo-web`, and `packages/contracts`. Installed workspace dependencies and validated `pnpm -r check` and `pnpm -r build`. Fixes required to make the moved workspace landable: removed `.ts` import suffixes in `packages/contracts` and `services/ninpo-api`, added a local `app/ninpo-web/postcss.config.mjs` to stop inheriting the main app Tailwind config, corrected a CH resolver field mismatch, and fixed nullable `siren` duplicate detection in the IP assets repository.
