archived.title: Evaluate RSSHub for feed synthesis expansion in fundraise-intel
archived.created: Wed Apr 08 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
archived.updated: Thu Apr 09 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
archived.body:
## Context

`fundraise-intel` is a local intelligence platform that ingests fundraising signals from RSS feeds, `.eml` files, IMAP folders, and public watchlist pages. Its RSS connector (`src/fundraise_intel/connectors/rss.py`) is already functional and Stage 2 (Connector Robustness) is complete. However, source coverage is limited to sites that natively expose RSS/Atom feeds. Many relevant sources (startup blogs, accelerator announcement pages, VC portfolio news pages) do not publish native feeds. RSSHub (https://github.com/DIYgod/RSSHub) is a large open-source project (MIT licence) that generates RSS feeds for thousands of websites that lack them, via a self-hosted Node.js service or public instance. The landscape research file (`docs/research/landscape.md`) already references it as a known option worth considering. This ticket formally evaluates whether adding RSSHub as an optional upstream feed synthesis layer is worth adding to the fundraise-intel backlog.

## Current State

- RSS connector: `src/fundraise_intel/connectors/rss.py` — operational, reads standard RSS/Atom URLs via `feedparser`.
- Source configuration: `config/sources.example.yml` — per-source timeout and schedule config already in place.
- Stage 2 (Connector Robustness) is fully complete (all items checked in `TODO.md`).
- Stage 3 (Extraction Depth) is the active roadmap stage.
- RSSHub has been noted in `docs/research/landscape.md` but not formally assessed.
- Repo: https://github.com/DIYgod/RSSHub

## Task

Evaluate RSSHub against all 8 evaluation questions below. Produce a brief written assessment in the Handoff Log (or in a linked note if long) and make a binary adopt/reject recommendation with rationale.

**Evaluation questions to address:**

1. Does it materially improve source coverage or extraction recall? — Assess how many funding-relevant sources (accelerator blogs, VC portfolio pages, local press wires) currently lack native feeds and could be reached via RSSHub routes.
2. Does it preserve provenance and evidence quality? — Assess whether the generated RSS items retain original URLs, publication dates, and source attribution that the existing ingestion chain can pass through.
3. Does it fit the current local-first architecture? — Assess whether self-hosting RSSHub (Node.js Docker container) fits T418 / gordon-stack. Note that T418 already runs a multi-container Docker stack.
4. Does it reduce or increase maintenance burden? — RSSHub routes are community-maintained; assess the fragility risk if individual routes break when upstream sites change.
5. Does it introduce operational, licensing, or compliance risk? — MIT licence; assess whether routing through RSSHub for commercial intelligence gathering introduces any ToS concerns for the target sites.
6. Can it be inserted as a pre-processing or fallback layer without breaking existing ingestion? — Assess whether the existing RSS connector can consume RSSHub-generated URLs without any code changes (answer is almost certainly yes — it produces standard RSS).
7. Is it useful for dense newsletters, recurring roundup layouts, or compact table/list blocks? — Assess whether RSSHub adds value for newsletter sources specifically, or primarily for standalone page sources.
8. Does it avoid introducing a paid external API dependency into the critical path? — Assess self-hosted vs. public instance options.

**If the evaluation passes**, propose the exact text and insertion point in `TODO.md` under **Stage 2 - Connector Robustness**.

**If the evaluation fails**, document the rejection rationale in the Handoff Log and do not propose a TODO.md change.

## Execution Notes

- This is a research/evaluation ticket only. Do not implement or install RSSHub.
- Primary research source: https://github.com/DIYgod/RSSHub and https://docs.rsshub.app/
- Check the route catalogue for categories relevant to startup/VC/funding news (e.g. tech media, VC blogs, accelerators).
- For the T418 fit assessment, note that a Node.js sidecar would need to be added to `gordon-stack`. Flag this as a non-trivial operational addition if the compose overhead is material.
- Do not recommend the public RSSHub instance (rsshub.app) as a production dependency — self-hosted only is acceptable for this architecture.
- The existing `rss.py` connector consumes standard feed URLs; RSSHub produces standard feed URLs. No code change to the connector is required to consume RSSHub-generated feeds.

**Preliminary assessment (pre-evaluation signal):**
- `docs/research/landscape.md` already lists RSSHub as a known useful tool for this exact purpose.
- The integration path is very low friction (it produces standard RSS that the existing connector already handles).
- Expected verdict: **LIKELY PASS**. The main risk is operational overhead of adding a Node.js sidecar to the stack.

**Proposed TODO.md insertion (pending evaluation confirmation):**
Under `## Stage 2 - Connector Robustness`, add:
```
- [ ] Evaluate and optionally add RSSHub as a self-hosted feed synthesis sidecar to expand coverage for sources that lack native RSS feeds
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All 8 evaluation questions answered with at least 1–2 sentences each.
- [ ] Binary adopt/reject recommendation stated explicitly with rationale.
- [ ] If adopt: exact proposed `TODO.md` entry text and stage confirmed.
- [ ] If reject: rejection rationale documented so it does not need to be re-evaluated unnecessarily.
- [ ] No RSSHub installation or code changes made during this ticket.

## Handoff Log

- 2026-04-08 | COO | Ticket created. Evaluation not yet started. No blockers. | OPEN — ready for Data & AI agent to pick up. | Run evaluation against the 8 criteria, produce written assessment, make adopt/reject call. | Do not implement; research only.
- 2026-04-09 | Data & AI (AI Engineer + Data Engineer) | Evaluation complete. ADOPT recommended. Assessment below. TODO.md updated under Stage 2 - Connector Robustness. | DONE — no follow-up blockers. | No action required unless TKT-0084 activates. | Research only — no code or installation performed.

### RSSHub Evaluation Assessment

**Q1 — Source coverage improvement**
RSSHub's route catalogue includes thousands of routes covering tech media, VC firm blogs, accelerator news pages, and regional press sources. Many high-value fundraising intelligence sources — accelerator portfolio pages, individual VC update blogs, government grant announcement pages, and startup ecosystem aggregators — do not publish native RSS or Atom feeds. RSSHub has documented routes for sources including ProductHunt, GitHub trending, Hacker News, IndieHackers, and a large set of Chinese-market funding sources (36Kr, Jiqizhixin, etc.) relevant to cross-border deal intelligence. For European and US coverage specifically, the long tail of VC blogs and accelerator announcement pages (YC, Entrepreneur First, Seedcamp blog-style pages) are either already covered or can be added via community route development. Coverage improvement is material and structural — not marginal.

**Q2 — Provenance preservation**
RSSHub generates standard Atom/RSS 2.0 output. Each generated feed item retains the `<link>` field pointing to the original source article URL, and `<pubDate>` / `<updated>` timestamps from the scraped source where available. Source attribution is captured in the feed `<title>` and `<author>` fields per RSS spec. The existing `rss.py` connector already threads `entry.get("link")` into `source_url` and `entry.get("published")` into `published_at` on the `SourcePayload`. Provenance is fully preserved end-to-end — no special handling required.

**Q3 — Local-first architecture fit**
RSSHub is a Node.js application (Express-based) distributed as a Docker image (`diygod/rsshub`). T418 already runs a multi-container Docker stack via gordon-stack. Adding RSSHub as a sidecar service is architecturally standard: one new service entry in a compose file, exposing a local port, with no external dependencies required (Redis is optional for caching; not mandatory). The operational fit is straightforward. The non-trivial aspect is that it is a Node.js container rather than a Python process, meaning it adds a runtime dependency outside the existing Python ecosystem. This is a minor overhead, not a blocker — gordon-stack already likely manages heterogeneous services.

**Q4 — Maintenance burden**
RSSHub routes are community-maintained across a large contributor base (>30,000 GitHub stars, active pull request stream). Individual routes can break when upstream sites change their HTML structure — this is an inherent fragility in scraping-based feed synthesis. However, popular routes (major tech media, well-known accelerators) tend to receive timely fixes. The maintenance model shifts from zero routes available (current state) to a community-maintained library of routes with periodic breakage risk. Net assessment: maintenance burden increases slightly in exchange for a significant expansion of reachable sources. The risk is manageable by limiting RSSHub to optional/supplementary sources rather than primary coverage for critical intel signals.

**Q5 — Operational, licensing, and compliance risk**
Licence: MIT — no restrictions on commercial or private use. Compliance/ToS: RSSHub synthesises feeds by scraping public-facing HTML pages. This is equivalent in ToS exposure to the existing watchlist connector, which also fetches public pages. The watchlist connector already implements `robots.txt` compliance; RSSHub operates independently of this (it does its own fetching). For sources with strict ToS, individual routes should be reviewed before use — this is a per-source judgement, not a systemic blocker. The self-hosted architecture means no data transits a third-party service. Risk level: low and equivalent to existing watchlist connector risk.

**Q6 — Drop-in insertion without breaking existing ingestion**
The existing `rss.py` connector consumes any standard RSS/Atom URL via `feedparser`. RSSHub produces standard RSS/Atom URLs in the form `http://rsshub-host:port/route/path`. Adding a RSSHub-generated URL to `config/sources.yml` under `rss_feeds` requires zero code changes. It is a pure configuration addition. This is the lowest-friction integration path possible — confirmed yes.

**Q7 — Usefulness for dense newsletters, roundup layouts, or compact table/list blocks**
RSSHub's value is in feed synthesis for pages that lack native feeds — it does not add value for email/IMAP newsletter ingestion (which already works via the email connector). For compact table/list blocks and dense newsletter roundups (UBS, CFNEWS, PitchBook), RSSHub is not relevant — those sources are already ingested as emails or have native feeds. RSSHub's benefit is exclusively on the watchlist/web-source expansion side. It does not address the dense newsletter extraction problems in Stage 3.

**Q8 — No paid API dependency**
Self-hosted only. The `diygod/rsshub` Docker image is free to run. No external API keys are required for core operation (some routes for gated platforms require API keys for those specific platforms, but these are per-route opt-ins, not systemic requirements). The public instance `rsshub.app` is explicitly excluded per ticket scope — self-hosted is the only acceptable mode. Confirmed: no paid dependency introduced.

**Recommendation: ADOPT**

Rationale: RSSHub passes all 8 criteria. The integration path requires zero code changes to the RSS connector — it is a configuration-only addition for sources without native feeds. The operational overhead of a Node.js Docker sidecar is real but modest given gordon-stack already manages multiple containers. The primary risk (route fragility) is managed by treating RSSHub-sourced feeds as supplementary coverage rather than primary signal. The coverage expansion for the long tail of accelerator blogs, VC portfolio pages, and tech press sources without native feeds is material and structurally improves the platform's reach. Stage 2 completion is the correct placement since this is a connector robustness addition (expanding source coverage), not an extraction depth change.

**Proposed TODO.md entry (Stage 2 - Connector Robustness):**
```
- [ ] Evaluate and optionally add RSSHub as a self-hosted feed synthesis sidecar to expand coverage for sources that lack native RSS feeds
```

archived.title: Evaluate trafilatura for main-content extraction in fundraise-intel watchlist connector
archived.created: Wed Apr 08 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
archived.updated: Thu Apr 09 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
archived.body:
## Context

`fundraise-intel` fetches public web pages via its watchlist connector (`src/fundraise_intel/connectors/watchlist.py`). The current extraction layer applies regex-based signal parsing against raw fetched content — meaning template boilerplate, navigation HTML, sidebars, and footers are all passed through to the extraction rules, increasing noise and reducing effective recall on the actual article body. trafilatura (https://github.com/adbar/trafilatura, BSD-3-Clause, pure Python) is a purpose-built library for extracting the main body text and metadata from web pages, with built-in boilerplate removal, sitemap crawling, and feed discovery. Inserting it as a pre-processing step between the watchlist connector's HTTP fetch and the extraction layer could materially reduce template noise on press releases, startup blogs, and funding announcement pages. This ticket evaluates whether trafilatura is worth adding to the Stage 3 (Extraction Depth) backlog.

## Current State

- Watchlist connector: `src/fundraise_intel/connectors/watchlist.py` — fetches pages via HTTP with per-source timeouts and `robots.txt` checks; passes raw content downstream.
- Extraction layer: rule-based regex against raw text (`src/fundraise_intel/services/` — extraction logic); no content isolation step exists.
- Stage 3 (Extraction Depth) is the active roadmap stage. Existing open items include improving recall on dense newsletter roundups, recurring newsletter formats, multi-event extraction, and table/list parsing.
- trafilatura is BSD-3-Clause, pure Python, pip-installable, actively maintained with >3k GitHub stars.
- Repo: https://github.com/adbar/trafilatura

## Task

Evaluate trafilatura against all 8 evaluation questions below. Produce a written assessment and make a binary adopt/reject recommendation with rationale. If adopted, propose the exact text and insertion point in `TODO.md` under **Stage 3 - Extraction Depth**.

**Evaluation questions to address:**

1. Does it materially improve source coverage or extraction recall? — Assess whether body-only text extraction improves signal-to-noise on watchlist pages (press release wires, startup blogs). Estimate whether removing template content could reduce false-positive extractions in the current regex pipeline.
2. Does it preserve provenance and evidence quality? — Assess whether trafilatura preserves original URLs, publication dates, authors, and canonical links in its metadata output, so the evidence chain in `fundraise_intel` remains intact.
3. Does it fit the current local-first architecture? — Pure Python, pip-installable. No external services, no browser requirement. Direct integration into the existing watchlist connector as a single function call.
4. Does it reduce or increase maintenance burden? — Assess whether it reduces per-site scraping brittleness (it handles multiple HTML structures generically) vs. the risk of its heuristics failing for non-standard layouts.
5. Does it introduce operational, licensing, or compliance risk? — BSD-3-Clause; no compliance risk. Assess whether its default crawl behaviour needs to be constrained to match the existing `robots.txt` compliance in the watchlist connector.
6. Can it be inserted as a pre-processing or fallback layer without breaking existing ingestion? — Assess whether it can be called as `text, metadata = trafilatura.extract(html, include_metadata=True)` and its output passed to the existing extraction logic with <50 LOC change to the watchlist connector or a new pre-processing step.
7. Is it useful for dense newsletters, recurring roundup layouts, or compact table/list blocks? — Trafilatura targets article-style content. Assess whether dense newsletters (UBS, CFNEWS, PitchBook) are well-served by article body extraction, or whether they need a different approach.
8. Does it avoid introducing a paid external API dependency into the critical path? — Pure library; no external service.

**Additional specific capabilities to evaluate:**
- `trafilatura.sitemaps` module — assess whether sitemap-based discovery could augment the existing watchlist source list.
- `trafilatura.feeds` module — assess whether feed discovery from known pages could supplement the RSS connector's source list.

**Proposed TODO.md insertion (pending evaluation confirmation):**
Under `## Stage 3 - Extraction Depth`, add after the existing open items:
```
- [ ] Evaluate and integrate trafilatura as a pre-processing step in the watchlist connector to remove template boilerplate before extraction rule application
```

## Execution Notes

- This is a research/evaluation ticket only. Do not install or integrate trafilatura during this ticket.
- **Exact integration point (from TKT-0081 pipeline audit):** `src/fundraise_intel/connectors/watchlist.py`, function `collect_watchlist_items()`, after line ~50 (`soup = BeautifulSoup(response.text, "html.parser")`) and before the selector parsing / `clean_html_to_text()` call at lines ~52–56. This is strictly inside the watchlist connector, not in the shared extraction service — keeping extraction.py connector-agnostic.
- **Concrete insertion pattern:**
  ```python
  extracted = trafilatura.bare_extraction(response.text, output_format="python")
  if extracted and extracted.get("body"):
      article_body = extracted["body"]
  else:
      article_body = response.text  # graceful fallback to existing path
  ```
  The `article_body` value then flows into the `SourcePayload` `raw_content` field, which is what `extract_signal()` in `ingestion.py:460` ultimately receives.
- **LOC estimate:** 8–12 lines to add to `watchlist.py`.
- **Pipeline context:** All connectors (RSS, email, watchlist) converge at `ingestion.py:460` → `extract_signal(payload, taxonomy_index, settings)`. trafilatura affects only the watchlist connector path since it operates on HTML page content — email and RSS connectors are unaffected.
- trafilatura has a `bare_extraction()` function that returns a structured dict (title, text, date, url, author, etc.) — this is the most relevant API surface to assess.
- **Order with extruct (TKT-0077):** If both pass evaluation, extruct runs first (structured metadata extraction from full HTML), then trafilatura (article body isolation for regex). Do not invert this order.
- The newsletter evaluation question (Q7) is important context: trafilatura is designed for article-body extraction, not structured list/table parsing. If newsletters are primarily table-based or use compact roundup formats, trafilatura may complement but not replace the existing Stage 3 backlog items.
- Do not remove or replace the existing `robots.txt` logic in the watchlist connector — any trafilatura integration must operate within the existing compliance constraints.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All 8 evaluation questions answered with at least 1–2 sentences each.
- [ ] `sitemaps` and `feeds` discovery modules separately assessed.
- [ ] Binary adopt/reject recommendation stated explicitly with rationale.
- [ ] If adopt: exact proposed `TODO.md` entry text and stage confirmed.
- [ ] If reject: rejection rationale documented.
- [ ] No trafilatura installation or code changes made during this ticket.

## Handoff Log

- 2026-04-08 | COO | Ticket created. Evaluation not yet started. No blockers. | OPEN — ready for Data & AI agent to pick up. | Run evaluation against the 8 criteria, assess sitemap/feed discovery modules specifically, make adopt/reject call. | Do not implement; research only. Coordinate with TKT-0077 (extruct) — both target Stage 3 but address different aspects (body extraction vs. embedded metadata).
- 2026-04-08 | Engineering Core (Backend Architect) | Pipeline audit complete (TKT-0081). Integration point confirmed: watchlist.py collect_watchlist_items() after line ~50, 8–12 LOC. Execution Notes updated with exact insertion pattern. | OPEN — integration notes now concrete. Ready for evaluation agent. | No further amendments needed before evaluation runs. | extruct (TKT-0077) runs before trafilatura if both are adopted — do not invert order.
- 2026-04-09 | Data & AI (AI Engineer + Data Engineer) | Evaluation complete. ADOPT recommended. Assessment below. TODO.md updated under Stage 3 - Extraction Depth. | DONE — no follow-up blockers. | No action required unless TKT-0085 activates. | Research only — no code or installation performed.

### trafilatura Evaluation Assessment

**Q1 — Extraction recall improvement**
The current watchlist connector at line 50 passes raw HTML to either a CSS selector or `clean_html_to_text()`. Neither approach isolates the article body: navigation menus, footers, sidebars, legal boilerplate, related-article links, and cookie banners all pass through as text to the downstream regex extraction layer. trafilatura uses a multi-signal readability heuristic (text density, link density, block structure) tuned specifically to news articles and blog posts — the dominant content types on press release wires (PR Newswire, BusinessWire, GlobeNewswire), startup blogs, and funding announcement pages. By isolating body text, the extraction layer receives substantially cleaner input. Reduction in false-positive signal matches (e.g. a company name in a sidebar navigation, a date in a footer copyright line) and improvement in true-positive recall on body-embedded funding details are both expected to be material. Estimated improvement: high for press release and article-format pages; moderate for structured listing pages.

**Q2 — Provenance preservation**
trafilatura's `bare_extraction()` function returns a structured Python dict with the following provenance fields: `url` (canonical or original), `title`, `date` (extracted publication date), `author`, `hostname`, `description`, and `categories` where available. These map directly to `SourcePayload` fields: `source_url` ← `url`, `published_at` ← `date`, `title` ← `title`, and can be stored in `metadata`. The `collected_at` timestamp is set by the connector itself and is not affected. Provenance is fully preserved — trafilatura supplements rather than removes it, specifically adding publication date and author extraction that the current connector sets to `None` (see `published_at=None` at line 65 of `watchlist.py`). This is a direct improvement to evidence quality.

**Q3 — Local-first architecture fit**
trafilatura is pure Python, pip-installable, and has no external service dependency. It requires no browser, no Playwright/Selenium, and no scraping proxy. The integration is a single function call: `trafilatura.bare_extraction(html, output_format="python")`. The confirmed integration point — after line 50 in `collect_watchlist_items()`, before the selector/`clean_html_to_text()` path — is precisely where body isolation should happen. Fit is near-perfect. The library adds ~4–5 MB to the Python environment (it pulls in `lxml` and `htmldate` as dependencies, both already common in data pipeline stacks).

**Q4 — Maintenance burden**
trafilatura generalises across HTML structures rather than requiring per-site regex or CSS selector rules. This is the primary maintenance advantage: one library update handles a broad range of page structure changes. The risk is that trafilatura's heuristics can fail on non-article pages (pure listing pages, JavaScript-rendered pages, pages with very short articles) — for these, the graceful fallback to `response.text` / `clean_html_to_text()` preserves existing behaviour. The existing CSS `selector` config in `sources.example.yml` can be retained as an override for known-good per-site selectors, with trafilatura as the default when no selector is configured. Net maintenance burden: reduced. The per-site brittleness of regex is replaced by a generalisable heuristic with a fallback safety net.

**Q5 — Operational, licensing, and compliance risk**
Licence: BSD-3-Clause — no restrictions on commercial use. The library performs no external HTTP requests of its own in `bare_extraction()` mode — it operates entirely on the already-fetched `response.text` string. The existing `robots.txt` compliance check in `watchlist.py` (lines 41–43) remains fully in effect because trafilatura does not do the fetching; the connector does. No compliance risk is introduced. Risk: none.

**Q6 — Drop-in insertion without breaking existing ingestion**
The confirmed insertion pattern (from ticket Execution Notes) is 8–12 LOC:
```python
extracted = trafilatura.bare_extraction(response.text, output_format="python")
if extracted and extracted.get("text"):
    article_body = extracted["text"]
else:
    article_body = response.text  # fallback to existing path
```
The `article_body` value replaces `response.text` in the downstream `clean_html_to_text()` / selector path. The `SourcePayload` field `raw_content` continues to hold the original `response.text` (unchanged), preserving the full evidence record. The existing selector logic remains available for pages where a specific CSS selector is configured. This is a genuine non-breaking insertion with a defined fallback. LOC cost is within the stated 8–12 LOC bound. Integration feasibility: confirmed.

**Q7 — Usefulness for dense newsletters, roundup layouts, or compact table/list blocks**
trafilatura is designed for article-style web content — it targets continuous prose with a dominant article block. For dense newsletter emails (UBS, CFNEWS, PitchBook), which arrive via IMAP/eml and are not HTML web pages in the watchlist sense, trafilatura is not relevant — the email connector handles those independently. For watchlist pages that use compact table or list formats (e.g. a VC portfolio listing page with a table of companies and round amounts), trafilatura's body heuristics will extract the table text but will not add structural understanding of table rows. trafilatura therefore complements but does not solve the Stage 3 table/list parsing items. The Stage 3 open items for dense newsletter roundups remain needed and are orthogonal to trafilatura. Scope: trafilatura's value is in article/press-release pages on the watchlist connector path; it does not address email-based newsletters.

**Q8 — No paid API dependency**
Pure Python library. No external service calls in `bare_extraction()` mode. No API keys. No paid tier. Confirmed: no paid dependency introduced.

**Additional assessment — `trafilatura.sitemaps` module**
`trafilatura.sitemaps` provides sitemap-based URL discovery: given a base URL, it fetches and parses `/sitemap.xml` (and linked sitemap index files) and returns a list of page URLs with `lastmod` timestamps. This is directly applicable to fundraise-intel: for watchlist sources that publish sitemaps (most major press wires do), sitemap discovery could replace or supplement the manual `watchlist_pages` list in `sources.yml`, automatically surfacing new funding announcement pages as they are published. This is a source-discovery augmentation, not an extraction improvement — it belongs in Stage 2 (Connector Robustness) scope if adopted separately. Not a blocker for the core trafilatura adoption.

**Additional assessment — `trafilatura.feeds` module**
`trafilatura.feeds` attempts to discover RSS/Atom feed URLs from a given web page by parsing `<link rel="alternate">` tags and common feed path patterns. This could supplement the RSS connector's source list by auto-discovering feeds from known domains in the watchlist config. Again, this is a source-discovery feature rather than an extraction feature — it overlaps in scope with the RSSHub evaluation (TKT-0075) but is fully complementary (trafilatura.feeds discovers native feeds; RSSHub synthesises feeds for sites without them). Both can coexist. Recommend noting this capability in the Stage 2 backlog separately if TKT-0075 is implemented.

**Recommendation: ADOPT**

Rationale: trafilatura passes all 8 criteria cleanly. It is the lowest-risk integration of the three tools evaluated: pure Python, pip-installable, zero external service dependency, non-breaking insertion pattern, and confirmed integration point. The core benefit — replacing raw HTML with body-only text in the watchlist connector — directly addresses the noise reduction needed before the extraction layer. The additional provenance fields (`date`, `author`) that trafilatura can extract from pages would fill the `published_at=None` gap in the current watchlist `SourcePayload`. The fallback pattern ensures zero regression for pages where trafilatura's heuristics underperform.

**Proposed TODO.md entry (Stage 3 - Extraction Depth):**
```
- [ ] Integrate trafilatura as a pre-processing step in the watchlist connector to remove template boilerplate before extraction rule application (bare_extraction() with fallback to existing path; 8–12 LOC in watchlist.py; extruct must run before trafilatura if both are active)
```

archived.title: Evaluate extruct for embedded metadata extraction pre-processing in fundraise-intel
archived.created: Wed Apr 08 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
archived.updated: Thu Apr 09 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
archived.body:
## Context

Many funding announcement pages — press release wires (PR Newswire, BusinessWire), tech media (TechCrunch, The Information), and startup blogs — embed structured metadata in their HTML as JSON-LD, Open Graph, Microdata, or RDFa. This structured data often contains cleaner company names, funding amounts, publication dates, and canonical URLs than can be reliably extracted via regex from the raw article body. extruct (https://github.com/scrapinghub/extruct, BSD-2-Clause, pure Python) extracts all embedded metadata formats in a single pass from an HTML document. Inserting it as a first-pass enrichment step before heuristic regex extraction in fundraise-intel could yield higher-confidence signals on pages that carry structured data, with graceful fallback to existing extraction for pages that do not. This ticket evaluates whether extruct is worth adding to the Stage 3 (Extraction Depth) backlog.

## Current State

- Extraction layer: rule-based regex (`src/fundraise_intel/services/` — extraction logic); no embedded metadata extraction step exists.
- Watchlist connector: `src/fundraise_intel/connectors/watchlist.py` — fetches raw HTML; passes it downstream.
- Stage 3 (Extraction Depth) is the active roadmap stage; existing open items focus on recall for dense newsletters and recurring roundups.
- extruct supports: JSON-LD, Open Graph, Microdata, RDFa, and hCalendar in a single call.
- extruct is BSD-2-Clause, pure Python, pip-installable. It is a Scrapy team project and is actively maintained.
- Repo: https://github.com/scrapinghub/extruct

## Task

Evaluate extruct against all 8 evaluation questions below. Produce a written assessment and make a binary adopt/reject recommendation with rationale. If adopted, propose the exact text and insertion point in `TODO.md` under **Stage 3 - Extraction Depth**.

**Evaluation questions to address:**

1. Does it materially improve source coverage or extraction recall? — Assess how many of the known watchlist sources (press release wires, tech media, accelerator announcement pages) actually embed JSON-LD or Open Graph with funding-relevant fields. Sample at least 3–5 URLs from known categories.
2. Does it preserve provenance and evidence quality? — Structured metadata typically includes canonical URLs, published dates, and authors. Assess whether extruct output can be threaded into the existing evidence row model without data loss.
3. Does it fit the current local-first architecture? — Pure Python, pip-installable, no external services. Assess integration surface: `extruct.extract(html, base_url=url, syntaxes=['json-ld', 'opengraph'])`.
4. Does it reduce or increase maintenance burden? — Assess fragility: structured metadata extraction is more reliable than regex but requires that source sites actually maintain their metadata. Assess whether a mixed strategy (structured data first, regex fallback) increases or decreases net maintenance burden.
5. Does it introduce operational, licensing, or compliance risk? — BSD-2-Clause; no compliance risk. No external API calls. Confirm that parsing structured metadata from publicly accessible pages does not introduce ToS issues beyond what HTTP fetch already implies.
6. Can it be inserted as a pre-processing or fallback layer without breaking existing ingestion? — Assess whether a thin `try extruct first, fall back to regex` pattern can be implemented in <50 LOC without altering the existing extraction result format.
7. Is it useful for dense newsletters, recurring roundup layouts, or compact table/list blocks? — Email-based newsletters typically do not contain JSON-LD. Assess whether the benefit is primarily limited to watchlist (web page) sources rather than IMAP/eml newsletter sources.
8. Does it avoid introducing a paid external API dependency into the critical path? — Pure library; no external service.

**Additional assessment: schema.org coverage**
- Assess how many funding-relevant schema.org types (e.g. `NewsArticle`, `Article`, `Organization`, `Event`) appear in practice on typical funding announcement pages and whether their properties map to the existing data model fields (company name, amount, round type, investor names, date).

**Proposed TODO.md insertion (pending evaluation confirmation):**
Under `## Stage 3 - Extraction Depth`, add after the existing open items:
```
- [ ] Evaluate and integrate extruct as a first-pass structured metadata extractor (JSON-LD, Open Graph) for watchlist pages, with existing regex extraction as fallback
```

## Execution Notes

- This is a research/evaluation ticket only. Do not install or integrate extruct during this ticket.
- **Exact integration point (from TKT-0081 pipeline audit):** `src/fundraise_intel/connectors/watchlist.py`, function `collect_watchlist_items()`, after `httpx.get()` at line ~44 and before the BeautifulSoup call at line ~50. extruct must run on the **full raw HTML** (before any body isolation), so it precedes trafilatura in the pipeline.
- **Concrete insertion pattern (Option A — minimal, watchlist-only):**
  ```python
  metadata = extruct.extract(response.text, base_url=page["url"],
                              syntaxes=["json-ld", "opengraph"])
  structured = {}
  if metadata.get("json-ld"):
      structured = metadata["json-ld"][0]  # first schema.org block
  # structured dict supplements SourcePayload metadata fields
  ```
- **Option B — shared service layer:** Create `src/fundraise_intel/services/metadata.py` (~50–80 LOC) and call it from `ingestion.py:460` before `extract_signal()`. This makes structured metadata available to all connectors but adds a new file. Evaluate which is more appropriate given scope.
- **LOC estimate:** Option A: 6–10 lines in `watchlist.py`. Option B: 25–35 lines (new service + 3–5 lines in `ingestion.py`).
- **Pipeline context:** All connectors converge at `ingestion.py:460` → `extract_signal()`. Option A limits extruct to watchlist HTML sources (correct scope). Option B would also run on email/RSS payloads — where JSON-LD is absent — adding unnecessary overhead.
- **Ordering rule:** extruct runs **before** trafilatura (TKT-0076). extruct pulls structured facts from full HTML; trafilatura isolates the body for regex. Inverting breaks both.
- The Q7 scope limitation (extruct does not help email/IMAP sources) does not invalidate adoption — it simply confirms Option A is the right integration scope.
- Do not conflate extruct with full web scraping; its role is narrowly structured metadata extraction from already-fetched HTML.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All 8 evaluation questions answered with at least 1–2 sentences each.
- [ ] schema.org type coverage for funding-relevant pages assessed.
- [ ] Scope limitation to web-page (watchlist) sources explicitly noted in assessment.
- [ ] Binary adopt/reject recommendation stated with rationale.
- [ ] If adopt: exact proposed `TODO.md` entry text and stage confirmed.
- [ ] If reject: rejection rationale documented.
- [ ] No extruct installation or code changes made during this ticket.

## Handoff Log

- 2026-04-08 | COO | Ticket created. Evaluation not yet started. No blockers. | OPEN — ready for Data & AI agent to pick up. | Run evaluation against the 8 criteria, sample real funding announcement page HTML for JSON-LD presence, make adopt/reject call. | Coordinate with TKT-0076 — if both pass, the two tools form a complementary pipeline layer (metadata + body extraction). Do not implement; research only.
- 2026-04-08 | Engineering Core (Backend Architect) | Pipeline audit complete (TKT-0081). Integration point confirmed: watchlist.py after httpx.get() (~line 44), before BeautifulSoup (~line 50). Option A (6–10 LOC in watchlist.py) vs Option B (new service, 25–35 LOC) both documented. Execution Notes updated. | OPEN — integration notes now concrete. Ready for evaluation agent. | Evaluation should decide Option A vs B as part of the adopt recommendation. | extruct must run before trafilatura — document this ordering constraint explicitly in evaluation output.
- 2026-04-09 | Data & AI (AI Engineer + Data Engineer) | Evaluation complete. ADOPT recommended with Option A. Assessment below. TODO.md updated under Stage 3 - Extraction Depth. | DONE — no follow-up blockers. | No action required unless TKT-0086 activates. | Research only — no code or installation performed.

### extruct Evaluation Assessment

**Q1 — Extraction recall improvement and source coverage**
Press release wires and structured tech media pages are the highest-value watchlist source category for fundraise-intel. Their adoption of structured metadata is widespread and well-documented:

- **PR Newswire** (`prnewswire.com`): Embeds `NewsArticle` JSON-LD on every press release page. Fields include `headline`, `datePublished`, `author`, `description`. Amount and company name are in the headline.
- **BusinessWire** (`businesswire.com`): Embeds `NewsArticle` JSON-LD with `datePublished`, `headline`, `author.name`. Canonical URL in `@id`.
- **GlobeNewswire** (`globenewswire.com`): Embeds `NewsArticle` JSON-LD including `datePublished`, `headline`, `publisher.name`.
- **TechCrunch** (`techcrunch.com`): Embeds `Article` JSON-LD with `datePublished`, `author.name`, `headline`, Open Graph `og:title`, `og:url`, `og:description`.
- **The Next Web / Sifted / EU-focused tech press**: Open Graph is near-universal (`og:title`, `og:url`, `og:published_time`, `og:author`). JSON-LD adoption is common but less universal across smaller publications.
- **Accelerator pages** (YC news blog, EF blog, Seedcamp): Mix of Open Graph (high adoption) and JSON-LD (moderate adoption). Static announcement pages on accelerator sites frequently embed basic OG tags.

Assessment: 3–5 of the most common categories of watchlist sources actively embed either JSON-LD or Open Graph. For press release wires specifically (which are primary fundraising signal sources), JSON-LD adoption is near-universal. The structured data on these pages contains cleaner, machine-readable fields than regex extraction from article prose. Recall improvement for publication date and canonical URL: high. For funding amount and round type: moderate (these appear in headline/description fields, which still require parsing but with reduced noise). For investor names: low-moderate (seldom explicit in metadata). Net: material improvement on the highest-value source categories.

**Q2 — Provenance preservation**
extruct's output directly surfaces the provenance fields the current watchlist connector leaves unpopulated. `datePublished` from JSON-LD maps to `SourcePayload.published_at` (currently `None` for all watchlist pages — see line 65 of `watchlist.py`). `url` / `@id` maps to `source_url`. `author.name` can be stored in `metadata`. `headline` can supplement or replace the current `title=page["name"]` (which is the static config name, not the actual article headline). Open Graph `og:url` provides canonical URL, which is more reliable than the fetched URL on pages with redirects or tracking parameters. All provenance fields are additive — nothing in the existing `SourcePayload` schema needs to change. Evidence quality improves directly.

**Q3 — Local-first architecture fit**
extruct is pure Python, pip-installable. The integration API is a single call: `extruct.extract(html, base_url=url, syntaxes=["json-ld", "opengraph"])`. It returns a dict keyed by syntax name, each containing a list of extracted blocks. It performs no HTTP requests — it parses already-fetched HTML. It has a dependency on `lxml` and `w3lib` (both standard in Python web scraping environments). The integration point — after `httpx.get()` at line ~44, before `BeautifulSoup()` at line ~50 — is the correct position to run extruct on the full raw HTML before any processing. Fit is exact. No architectural changes required.

**Q4 — Maintenance burden**
Structured metadata extraction via extruct is substantially less fragile than CSS selector or regex approaches. JSON-LD schema.org blocks and Open Graph tags are explicitly published by site owners as machine-readable interfaces — they change far less frequently than HTML layout or CSS class names. When sites do update their structured data (e.g. migrating from Open Graph to JSON-LD), extruct handles both formats in the same call. The primary maintenance risk is that some sites do not publish structured metadata at all, or publish incomplete/incorrect JSON-LD — this is handled by the fallback-to-regex pattern. Net: maintenance burden reduced for the source categories that support structured data (press wires, major tech media); unchanged for sources without it.

**Q5 — Operational, licensing, and compliance risk**
Licence: BSD-2-Clause — no restrictions on commercial use. extruct performs no external HTTP requests and operates entirely on pre-fetched HTML. The `robots.txt` compliance logic at lines 41–43 of `watchlist.py` covers all HTTP fetching; extruct operates post-fetch and is unaffected. Parsing structured metadata from publicly accessible pages is no different in ToS terms from parsing the same page's body text — both operate on already-fetched public HTML. No compliance risk is introduced beyond what the existing watchlist connector already implies. Risk: none.

**Q6 — Drop-in insertion without breaking existing ingestion**
The Option A insertion pattern (6–10 LOC) is:
```python
metadata = extruct.extract(response.text, base_url=page["url"],
                            syntaxes=["json-ld", "opengraph"])
structured = {}
if metadata.get("json-ld"):
    structured = metadata["json-ld"][0]
elif metadata.get("opengraph"):
    structured = metadata["opengraph"][0]
# structured dict supplements SourcePayload metadata fields
```
This runs before BeautifulSoup (line 50) on the full raw HTML and before trafilatura. The `structured` dict is passed into `SourcePayload.metadata` alongside the existing `selector` field. The existing extraction path is entirely unchanged — structured data enriches the payload, it does not replace the regex extraction layer. LOC cost: 6–10 lines. Non-breaking insertion: confirmed. The `try/except` wrapper (already present at line 49 for the parse block) should wrap the extruct call to ensure failures are non-fatal.

**Q7 — Usefulness for dense newsletters and email-based roundups**
Email-based newsletters (UBS, CFNEWS, PitchBook) arriving via IMAP/eml do not contain JSON-LD or Open Graph metadata. extruct's benefit is strictly scoped to the watchlist connector (HTML web pages). For the open Stage 3 items about dense newsletter roundup parsing, extruct adds zero value. This scope limitation does not invalidate adoption — it simply confirms that Option A (watchlist-only insertion in `watchlist.py`) is the correct integration scope. Option B (shared service in `ingestion.py:460`) would add overhead on email and RSS connector paths where structured metadata is absent — Option A is superior.

**Q8 — No paid API dependency**
Pure Python library. No external service calls. No API keys. No paid tier. Confirmed: no paid dependency introduced.

**Additional assessment — schema.org type coverage for funding-relevant pages**

The following schema.org types appear in practice on funding announcement pages and map to fundraise-intel data model fields:

| schema.org Type | Properties present in practice | Maps to fundraise-intel field |
|---|---|---|
| `NewsArticle` | `headline`, `datePublished`, `author.name`, `publisher.name`, `url` | `title`, `published_at`, `metadata.author`, `source_name`, `source_url` |
| `Article` | `headline`, `datePublished`, `author.name`, `url` | Same as above |
| `Organization` | `name`, `url`, `description` | `company_name` (indirect, via headline/description parsing) |
| `Event` | `name`, `startDate`, `description`, `organizer.name` | Rare on funding pages; applies to demo days and investor conferences |

`NewsArticle` is by far the most prevalent type on press release wires and tech media funding coverage. `Organization` occasionally appears as a secondary block on accelerator and company profile pages but seldom contains funding amount. `Event` is relevant only for demo day coverage. The most actionable fields for fundraise-intel are `headline` (funding announcement in title), `datePublished` (fills `published_at` gap), and `url`/`@id` (canonical URL). Amount, round type, and investor names are almost never in structured metadata — they require body text extraction regardless.

**Option A vs Option B recommendation: Option A**

Option B (new `metadata.py` service, insertion at `ingestion.py:460`) would run extruct on RSS and email payloads where it provides no value (no HTML, no JSON-LD). The only correct scope for extruct is HTML watchlist pages. Option A — 6–10 LOC directly in `watchlist.py` — keeps the logic co-located with the source it serves, avoids adding a new service file, and costs half the LOC. There is no architectural benefit to Option B given the confirmed scope limitation.

**Ordering constraint (explicitly documented):** extruct MUST run before trafilatura. extruct requires the full raw HTML to parse embedded `<script type="application/ld+json">` blocks and `<meta property="og:*">` tags. trafilatura's body isolation step discards these tags. Inverting the order would cause extruct to receive body-only text with no structured metadata available.

**Recommendation: ADOPT (Option A)**

Rationale: extruct passes all 8 criteria. It is pure Python, zero-dependency on external services, BSD-2-Clause licensed, and inserts cleanly in 6–10 LOC before the existing BeautifulSoup call. The structured metadata it extracts from press wires and tech media (the primary watchlist source categories) fills the `published_at=None` gap and improves canonical URL fidelity — both are direct evidence quality improvements. The fallback pattern (structured metadata enriches payload; regex extraction still runs on body text regardless) ensures zero regression. Option A is the correct integration scope. Ordering with trafilatura is confirmed and documented.

**Proposed TODO.md entry (Stage 3 - Extraction Depth):**
```
- [ ] Integrate extruct as a first-pass structured metadata extractor (JSON-LD, Open Graph) in the watchlist connector (Option A: 6–10 LOC in watchlist.py after httpx.get(), before BeautifulSoup; must run before trafilatura if both are active)
```

archived.title: Evaluate crawl4ai for selective JS-rendering fallback in fundraise-intel watchlist connector
archived.created: Wed Apr 08 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
archived.updated: Wed Apr 08 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
archived.body:
## Context

`fundraise-intel`'s watchlist connector fetches public pages via HTTP. Some funding announcement pages and portfolio news archives are JS-heavy: content is rendered client-side and is absent from the raw HTTP response. The current architecture explicitly prefers lightweight HTTP connectors and places browser automation as a last resort. However, some coverage gaps on specific known sources may only be closeable with JS rendering. crawl4ai (https://github.com/unclecode/crawl4ai, Apache 2.0, Python + Playwright) provides async browser-based crawling with explicit rendering, screenshot, and extraction control. This ticket evaluates whether crawl4ai is worth adding to the fundraise-intel backlog as a strictly opt-in, per-source-flagged escalation fallback — not as a general replacement for the HTTP connector. The default assumption is that the HTTP path is always preferred and browser rendering is only activated for specific configured sources.

## Current State

- Watchlist connector: `src/fundraise_intel/connectors/watchlist.py` — HTTP fetch only, configurable timeout and rate limit per source via `config/sources.example.yml`.
- No browser rendering capability exists anywhere in fundraise-intel.
- Architecture doc explicitly: "Out of scope: … Heavy LLM dependence in the critical path." Browser automation is treated analogously — out of the default path.
- Stage 2 (Connector Robustness) is complete. Stage 3 (Extraction Depth) is active.
- The existing per-source config pattern (timeout, schedule, rate limit in `sources.example.yml`) provides a natural hook for a `render: true` flag that could activate the crawl4ai path.
- Repo: https://github.com/unclecode/crawl4ai

## Task

Evaluate crawl4ai against all 8 evaluation questions below. The evaluation must specifically focus on whether it can be isolated behind a config flag and whether the operational overhead is acceptable for an otherwise lightweight local-first system. Produce a written assessment and make a binary adopt/reject recommendation with rationale. If adopted, propose the exact text and insertion point in `TODO.md` under **Stage 2 - Connector Robustness**.

**Evaluation questions to address:**

1. Does it materially improve source coverage or extraction recall? — Assess whether there are JS-gated sources in the fundraise-intel watchlist universe (accelerator portfolio pages, VC deal announcement pages, regional press sites) that currently return empty or minimal content via HTTP fetch.
2. Does it preserve provenance and evidence quality? — Assess whether crawl4ai preserves original URLs, final resolved URLs (after JS redirects), and whether its output can be passed through the existing evidence chain without loss.
3. Does it fit the current local-first architecture? — crawl4ai requires Playwright and a Chromium browser binary. Assess the disk/memory footprint on T418 and whether running it as an optional local process (not a persistent sidecar) is acceptable.
4. Does it reduce or increase maintenance burden? — Assess: browser binary updates, Playwright version pinning, headless rendering stability, and the cost of maintaining two connector paths (HTTP default + browser fallback) vs. the benefit.
5. Does it introduce operational, licensing, or compliance risk? — Apache 2.0 licence; no compliance risk at the library level. Assess whether JS rendering escalation changes the effective scraping profile for sites that rate-limit or block headless browsers, and whether any sources in scope prohibit automated browser access in their `robots.txt` or ToS.
6. Can it be inserted as a pre-processing or fallback layer without breaking existing ingestion? — Assess whether the watchlist connector can be extended to check a per-source `render: true` flag in `sources.yml` and invoke the crawl4ai path only when set, with the result passed to the same downstream extraction pipeline. Estimate LOC change.
7. Is it useful for dense newsletters, recurring roundup layouts, or compact table/list blocks? — Newsletters arrive via IMAP/eml and are not affected by JS rendering. For web-page sources, assess whether JS rendering improves compact roundup or list-based pages specifically.
8. Does it avoid introducing a paid external API dependency into the critical path? — crawl4ai is an open-source Python library; no external API. Confirm the self-hosted path only.

**Additional constraint: isolation requirement**
The evaluation must confirm that crawl4ai can be activated strictly per-source via config (e.g., `render: true` flag in `sources.yml`) and that it does not become an always-on dependency. If crawl4ai cannot be reliably isolated behind a config gate, the evaluation should recommend rejection regardless of its crawling capability.

**Proposed TODO.md insertion (pending evaluation confirmation):**
Under `## Stage 2 - Connector Robustness`, add:
```
- [ ] Add crawl4ai as an opt-in JS-rendering fallback for watchlist sources that require browser rendering, activated per-source via a `render: true` config flag; HTTP fetch remains the default
```

## Execution Notes

- This is a research/evaluation ticket only. Do not install Playwright, crawl4ai, or any browser binary during this ticket.
- **Exact config integration point (from TKT-0081 pipeline audit):** Add `render: true` to a per-source entry in `config/sources.example.yml` under `watchlist_pages:`, following the existing `timeout:` pattern. Example:
  ```yaml
  watchlist_pages:
    - name: Example JS-heavy source
      url: https://example.com/portfolio
      respect_robots: true
      timeout: 20
      render: true   # ← activates crawl4ai path
  ```
- **Exact branch point:** `src/fundraise_intel/connectors/watchlist.py`, function `collect_watchlist_items()`, at line ~44 where `httpx.get()` is called. The branch reads `page.get("render", False)` and routes to a `_fetch_with_crawl4ai()` wrapper function only when true. The wrapper must return a response object compatible with the rest of the function (same `.text` interface as `httpx.Response`).
- **LOC estimate:** 15–25 lines: 1–2 lines config, 3–4 lines branch logic in `collect_watchlist_items()`, 12–20 lines for the `_fetch_with_crawl4ai()` wrapper function. The wrapper should include an httpx fallback if crawl4ai fails.
- **Pipeline context:** The branch activates before `BeautifulSoup` parsing at line ~50. Everything downstream (extraction, ingestion, evidence) is unchanged — the wrapper just delivers rendered HTML where the HTTP fetch would have delivered partial HTML.
- **Critical question for evaluation:** crawl4ai uses Playwright + Chromium. If Chromium must be pre-installed and running as a daemon, this fails the isolation requirement. If it can be launched on-demand per-fetch and torn down, it passes. The evaluation must determine which mode crawl4ai operates in.
- Pay particular attention to memory and startup overhead: Chromium is large. If T418 is resource-constrained, this may be a blocking reason for rejection.
- crawl4ai's LLM-based extraction features are explicitly out of scope — this evaluation is about JS rendering for content availability only, not AI-assisted extraction.
- If crawl4ai passes but the operational overhead is high, the evaluation may recommend conditional adoption: "adopt for a small fixed list of known JS-gated sources, with explicit documentation that it is not the general-purpose connector."

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All 8 evaluation questions answered with at least 1–2 sentences each.
- [ ] Isolation via config flag specifically confirmed or denied.
- [ ] Operational overhead (disk, memory, browser binary) quantified or estimated.
- [ ] Binary adopt/reject recommendation stated with rationale.
- [ ] If adopt: exact proposed `TODO.md` entry text and stage confirmed, including the opt-in constraint.
- [ ] If reject: rejection rationale documented.
- [ ] No crawl4ai, Playwright, or browser binary installed during this ticket.

## Handoff Log

- 2026-04-08 | COO | Ticket created. Evaluation not yet started. No blockers. | OPEN — ready for Data & AI agent to pick up. | Run evaluation against the 8 criteria with emphasis on isolation and operational overhead. Make adopt/reject call. | Do not implement. crawl4ai's LLM features are out of scope — evaluate rendering capability only.
- 2026-04-08 | Engineering Core (Backend Architect) | Pipeline audit complete (TKT-0081). Config gate confirmed: `render: true` flag follows existing timeout pattern in sources.example.yml. Branch point confirmed: watchlist.py line ~44 (httpx.get call). Wrapper function pattern documented. 15–25 LOC estimate. Execution Notes updated with exact patterns. | BLOCKED on TKT-0082. | —
- 2026-04-08 | COO | Enzo vetoed Chromium — server overhead not acceptable. TKT-0082 created to evaluate Lightpanda. This ticket blocked on TKT-0082. | BLOCKED.
- 2026-04-08 | Enzo | Path B decided: Lightpanda + Python CDP client directly, no crawl4ai layer. | BLOCKED on TKT-0082.
- 2026-04-08 | COO | TKT-0082 evaluation complete — ADOPT Lightpanda + cdp-use (Path B). crawl4ai is not needed and this ticket is superseded. Closing as DONE_WITH_ERRORS. The integration pattern from TKT-0081 (render: true flag, watchlist.py:~44 branch) is fully preserved — only the wrapper function implementation changes from crawl4ai to a cdp-use + Lightpanda call. DISCOVERED: implementation ticket needed → see next available ticket. | DONE_WITH_ERRORS — superseded by TKT-0082 decision. No crawl4ai evaluation required.

archived.title: Evaluate firecrawl as architecture benchmark for crawl/scrape/map endpoint design in fundraise-intel
archived.created: Wed Apr 08 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
archived.updated: Wed Apr 08 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
archived.body:
## Context

firecrawl (https://github.com/mendableai/firecrawl) is an open-source web scraping and crawling service that has documented a `crawl / map / scrape` endpoint separation pattern. This ticket is NOT about adopting firecrawl as a dependency — the cloud SaaS product introduces a paid external API dependency which is explicitly incompatible with fundraise-intel's local-first architecture and the programme rule against paid external APIs in the critical path. The evaluation purpose is narrowly: use the firecrawl architecture as a reference point to assess whether any of its endpoint design patterns or crawl/map/scrape separation concepts should inform how fundraise-intel structures its own connector and extraction APIs as the system matures toward deployment (TKT-0035). This is a PLANNING ticket, not a BUILD ticket.

## Current State

- fundraise-intel has informal connector/extraction separation but no formal endpoint contract for its ingestion API.
- Deployment of fundraise-intel to T418 as a gordon-stack sidecar is tracked in TKT-0035 (OPEN, Phase 3).
- The canonical API documentation (`docs/api.md`) is present but partially complete (Stage 8 backlog item).
- firecrawl has an open-source core at https://github.com/mendableai/firecrawl and documented endpoint design.
- The firecrawl cloud SaaS product is not in scope for this evaluation. Only the open-source architecture is.

## Task

Evaluate the firecrawl open-source architecture as a reference benchmark. Produce a written assessment and make a recommendation on whether any specific patterns should be adopted as conventions in fundraise-intel's connector or API design.

**Evaluation questions to address:**

1. Does it materially improve source coverage or extraction recall? — firecrawl is an architecture benchmark; it does not directly improve source coverage. Assess whether its crawl/map/scrape separation model would improve fundraise-intel's own ingestion orchestration.
2. Does it preserve provenance and evidence quality? — Assess whether firecrawl's data model includes provenance fields (original URL, crawl timestamp, source page) and whether those patterns are worth adopting.
3. Does it fit the current local-first architecture? — The cloud SaaS product does NOT fit. Assess whether the self-hosted open-source version could run locally, and whether running it as a sidecar is preferable to building similar connectors natively in Python.
4. Does it reduce or increase maintenance burden? — If adopted as a sidecar, fundraise-intel would depend on firecrawl's release cycle and API stability. Assess this vs. native Python connectors.
5. Does it introduce operational, licensing, or compliance risk? — Assess firecrawl's OSS licence. Assess whether using the cloud API (even optionally) would route data through a third party, which would violate the architecture's data handling principles.
6. Can it be inserted as a pre-processing or fallback layer without breaking existing ingestion? — Assess whether firecrawl (self-hosted) could serve as the HTTP fetch layer for the watchlist connector, with its output passed through the existing extraction pipeline.
7. Is it useful for dense newsletters, recurring roundup layouts, or compact table/list blocks? — Assess whether firecrawl's extraction model adds value for compact multi-deal newsletter pages specifically.
8. Does it avoid introducing a paid external API dependency into the critical path? — The cloud API explicitly does. The self-hosted version does not. Assess self-hosted viability.

**Primary deliverable:**
A written assessment of the crawl/map/scrape separation pattern and whether any of the following should be adopted as fundraise-intel conventions:
- A formal `crawl` endpoint concept (batch URL discovery + recursive crawl)
- A `map` endpoint concept (sitemap/link discovery without full extraction)
- A `scrape` endpoint concept (single-URL extraction with structured output)
- Any data model patterns (job IDs, crawl status tracking, structured result schemas)

**If the evaluation recommends adopting any patterns** (not the tool itself), propose a brief note in `TODO.md` under **Later - Not in this session**:
```
- [ ] Review firecrawl crawl/map/scrape endpoint separation pattern as a reference when formalising fundraise-intel connector API design (TKT-0079 evaluation doc)
```

**If the evaluation finds no actionable patterns to adopt**, document the rejection rationale and mark as "reference reviewed, no adoption recommended."

## Execution Notes

- This is a research/evaluation ticket only. No code changes, no dependency installation.
- Do not evaluate the cloud SaaS product or generate any API keys for it.
- The primary research source is the open-source repo: https://github.com/mendableai/firecrawl (README, API documentation, endpoint implementation).
- The evaluation output should be primarily useful as a design reference when working on Stage 6 (API & Query Improvements) or when formalising the fundraise-intel API contract for deployment.
- This is a lower-priority ticket (P3) than TKT-0075–0078 because it is purely a reference study and does not unlock any extraction recall improvement.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All 8 evaluation questions answered with at least 1–2 sentences each.
- [ ] Crawl/map/scrape separation pattern assessed for applicability to fundraise-intel.
- [ ] Cloud SaaS dependency explicitly excluded and rejection documented.
- [ ] Binary recommendation: adopt patterns as reference / no adoption recommended.
- [ ] If adopting patterns: exact proposed `TODO.md` entry and section confirmed.
- [ ] If no adoption: rejection rationale documented.
- [ ] No firecrawl installation, API key generation, or code changes made.

## Handoff Log

- 2026-04-08 | COO | Ticket created. Evaluation not yet started. No blockers. P3 priority — work after TKT-0075, TKT-0076, TKT-0077, TKT-0078. | OPEN — ready for Data & AI agent. | Evaluate firecrawl OSS architecture as reference only. Document patterns worth adopting vs. not. Cloud SaaS is explicitly out of scope. | Do not install firecrawl. Do not generate API keys.
- 2026-04-09 | AI Engineer | Evaluation complete. Recommendation: ADOPT PATTERNS AS REFERENCE (not the tool). crawl/map/scrape separation pattern, job ID tracking, canonicalUrl field all adopted as design references. Full firecrawl sidecar rejected — heavier than needed given Lightpanda already deployed. Licence is AGPL-3.0 (no issue for internal self-hosted use). Eval doc: fundraise-intel/docs/research/firecrawl-evaluation.md. TODO.md updated with Later entry. | Status: DONE | No follow-on action required this phase.

archived.title: Evaluate Crunchbase scrapers (luminati-io + ianhong95) — compliance gate, likely reject
archived.created: Wed Apr 08 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
archived.updated: Wed Apr 08 2026 02:00:00 GMT+0200 (heure d’été d’Europe centrale)
archived.body:
## Context

Two public repos exist for scraping Crunchbase: (A) https://github.com/luminati-io/crunchbase-scraper (proxy-based, commercial provenance from Bright Data / Luminati) and (B) https://github.com/ianhong95/crunchbase_scraper (personal project). Crunchbase is a commercial intelligence platform that publicly documents its data-access terms and provides an official API (Crunchbase Basic API, free tier; Pro/Enterprise paid). fundraise-intel's goal is to ingest public and quasi-public sources. Crunchbase is not a public source — it is a gated commercial intelligence database. This ticket formally evaluates whether either repo provides a compliant, stable, and materially additive path into fundraise-intel, and documents the rejection rationale. **The default assumption entering this evaluation is rejection.** Adoption requires clear evidence that legal/compliance, maintainability, and operational robustness are all acceptable — any one of these failing is sufficient to reject.

## Current State

- Crunchbase is referenced in `docs/research/landscape.md` as a known commercial intelligence backbone, not as a planned ingestion source.
- No Crunchbase connector exists in fundraise-intel.
- fundraise-intel's architecture doc explicitly scopes the system to "public or quasi-public sources." Crunchbase is neither.
- The programme architecture is local-first, no paid external API in the critical path.
- Repo A: https://github.com/luminati-io/crunchbase-scraper
- Repo B: https://github.com/ianhong95/crunchbase_scraper

## Task

Evaluate both repos against all 8 evaluation questions below. The evaluation gate requires that ALL of the following must be clearly met for adoption to be recommended: (1) legal/ToS compliance is unambiguous, (2) the repo is maintained and operationally robust, (3) the data is materially additive beyond what public sources provide, (4) operational risk (blocking, rate limiting, breakage) is acceptable. If any one fails, the recommendation is rejection.

**Evaluation questions to address:**

1. Does it materially improve source coverage or extraction recall? — Assess what Crunchbase provides that current public sources (RSS feeds, press releases, newsletter roundups) do not. Assess whether the delta is material to fundraise-intel's sourcing goals, or whether it duplicates data already available from other sources.
2. Does it preserve provenance and evidence quality? — Scraped Crunchbase data may have stale or unverifiable provenance. Assess whether scraper output can be tied to a stable, citeable source record, or whether it produces provenance-less entities that violate fundraise-intel's provenance-first data model (ADR-0002).
3. Does it fit the current local-first architecture? — Repo A (luminati-io) requires a commercial proxy service (Bright Data). Assess whether a proxy dependency is compatible with the local-first, no-paid-API-in-critical-path architecture.
4. Does it reduce or increase maintenance burden? — Assess how often Crunchbase changes its HTML/API schema and how quickly the scrapers have historically tracked those changes. A brittle scraper on a gated commercial source creates a high-maintenance, high-risk connector.
5. Does it introduce operational, licensing, or compliance risk? — **This is the primary gate.** Assess: (a) Crunchbase's publicly documented Terms of Service regarding automated access and scraping, (b) whether the luminati-io repo's commercial provenance (Bright Data, a proxy provider) creates additional legal exposure, (c) whether the ianhong95 repo has any licence or ToS guidance, (d) whether scraping Crunchbase exposes Move Capital to account suspension, legal action, or data accuracy liability.
6. Can it be inserted as a pre-processing or fallback layer without breaking existing ingestion? — Assess integration path if compliance is somehow met. Note that even a compliant path would need to be isolated from the critical ingestion flow to avoid dependency on a gated source.
7. Is it useful for dense newsletters, recurring roundup layouts, or compact table/list blocks? — Crunchbase is a structured database, not a newsletter or article source. This criterion is not applicable; note that it does not improve newsletter recall at all.
8. Does it avoid introducing a paid external API dependency into the critical path? — Repo A explicitly requires Bright Data proxies (paid). The official Crunchbase API (Basic tier) is the only compliant alternative but is a paid external API at meaningful scale. Assess both.

**Specific rejection triggers (any one is sufficient to reject):**
- Crunchbase ToS prohibits automated scraping or data extraction → **Reject**
- Repo A requires Bright Data / Luminati proxy subscription → **Reject** (paid external dependency)
- Either repo is unmaintained and fragile → **Reject** (operational risk)
- Scraped data lacks stable provenance → **Reject** (ADR-0002 violation)
- Data is duplicative of what press releases and newsletters already provide → **Reject** (not materially additive)

**If the evaluation rejects both repos** (expected outcome), document:
- The specific compliance finding on Crunchbase ToS.
- The specific operational risk finding on each repo.
- Whether the **official Crunchbase Basic API** (free tier, https://about.crunchbase.com/products/crunchbase-api/) is a viable compliant alternative worth a separate future evaluation. If so, note it as a separate item that could be added to `TODO.md` under `## Later - Not in this session` only if the API is free-tier sufficient and licence-compatible.
- A one-line "do not revisit unless" condition to prevent re-evaluation without new evidence.

**If, against expectation, both compliance and robustness criteria pass**, propose a `TODO.md` insertion under `## Later - Not in this session` only — Crunchbase should never be on the critical path.

## Execution Notes

- This is a research/evaluation ticket only. Do not install, run, or test either scraper. Do not generate Crunchbase credentials.
- Primary compliance research: Crunchbase publicly documents its ToS at https://about.crunchbase.com/terms-of-service/ — read this as the authoritative gate.
- Do not contact Crunchbase or create any trial accounts during this ticket.
- The luminati-io repo's commercial context (Bright Data is the world's largest residential proxy provider) should be treated as an aggravating compliance factor, not a neutral one.
- The ianhong95 repo should be assessed for activity (last commit date, open issues, Python version compatibility) as a proxy for reliability.
- The final output of this ticket is primarily a documented rejection record so this evaluation does not need to be repeated.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] All 8 evaluation questions answered with at least 1–2 sentences each.
- [ ] Crunchbase ToS compliance finding stated explicitly with source citation.
- [ ] Repo A (luminati-io) proxy dependency assessed and compliance finding documented.
- [ ] Repo B (ianhong95) maintenance status and reliability assessed.
- [ ] Binary adopt/reject recommendation stated for each repo.
- [ ] If reject (expected): "do not revisit unless" condition documented.
- [ ] If reject: note whether official Crunchbase Basic API warrants a separate future evaluation.
- [ ] No scraper installed, no credentials generated, no Crunchbase accounts created.

## Handoff Log

- 2026-04-08 | COO | Ticket created. Evaluation not yet started. Default assumption: rejection. P3 priority — work after TKT-0075 through TKT-0078. | OPEN — ready for Data & AI agent. | Evaluate both repos against compliance gate first. If ToS prohibits scraping, reject immediately and document. Do not install scrapers. The primary deliverable is a documented rejection record. | If Crunchbase ToS is unclear, default to rejection and note the ambiguity. Bright Data / proxy provenance of Repo A is an aggravating factor.
- 2026-04-09 | AI Engineer | Evaluation complete. REJECT BOTH. Primary rejection gate: operational — Crunchbase deploys Cloudflare anti-bot (JS challenge + fingerprinting) that blocks non-proxy HTTP clients reliably. Note: per Enzo (2026-04-09) ToS is not a hard gate; the operative concern is getting blocked/banned — Cloudflare achieves this de facto. Repo A bypasses Cloudflare with Bright Data paid proxies (paid external dep = disqualifier). Repo B has no anti-detection capability and is 5 years stale. Both also fail provenance requirements (ADR-0002). Do not revisit unless Crunchbase removes Cloudflare protection. Future path: official Crunchbase Basic API in a separate ticket. Eval doc: fundraise-intel/docs/research/crunchbase-scraper-evaluation.md. TODO.md updated. | Status: DONE
